commands.tooltip: Lists USB devices with detailed technical information about each one.

commands.desc: Display memory hardware information from BIOS
commands.tooltip: Reads memory hardware information from the system BIOS/firmware — shows RAM type, speed, and installed slots.

commands.desc: Display processor information from BIOS DMI table
commands.tooltip: Reads CPU information from the system BIOS/firmware, often more detailed than /proc/cpuinfo.

commands.desc: Show free and used memory in human-readable format
commands.tooltip: Shows how much RAM is free and in use. -h shows sizes in human-readable units (K, M, G).

commands.desc: Show memory usage in megabytes
commands.tooltip: Shows memory usage with sizes in megabytes.

commands.desc: Report virtual memory, processes, I/O, CPU statistics
commands.tooltip: Displays a snapshot of system statistics: CPU activity, memory, swap, I/O, and processes.

commands.desc: Print vmstat 5 times every 1 second
commands.tooltip: Prints vmstat output 5 times, once per second — watches system stats over time.

commands.desc: Report CPU and disk I/O statistics
commands.tooltip: Shows CPU usage statistics and disk I/O (input/output) statistics for each disk.

commands.desc: Extended disk statistics refreshed every second
commands.tooltip: Shows extended disk I/O statistics with more detail, refreshed every second.

commands.desc: Report CPU utilization every 1 second, 5 times
commands.tooltip: Reports CPU utilization every second for 5 intervals. sar collects and reports system activity.

commands.desc: Show per-CPU statistics every 1 second
commands.tooltip: Shows CPU statistics for each individual core/processor every second. Helpful for identifying uneven CPU load.

commands.desc: Versatile tool showing combined system resource stats
commands.tooltip: A versatile monitoring tool that shows CPU, memory, disk, and network stats all at once in real time.

commands.desc: Display full system information summary
commands.tooltip: Displays a comprehensive summary of all system hardware and software information in one command.

commands.desc: Print the number of available processing units
commands.tooltip: Prints how many CPU processing units (cores/threads) are available to the current process.

commands.desc: Show whether OS is 32-bit or 64-bit
commands.tooltip: Shows whether the operating system is running in 32-bit or 64-bit mode.

commands.desc: Display detailed CPU info from kernel proc filesystem
commands.tooltip: Shows raw CPU information from the Linux kernel's virtual filesystem, including model name, cores, and cache.

commands.desc: Display detailed memory info from proc filesystem
commands.tooltip: Shows raw memory information from the Linux kernel, including total, free, available, and cached RAM.

commands.desc: Show Linux distribution information
commands.tooltip: Shows what Linux distribution and version is installed, including the version number and codename.

commands.desc: Display Linux Standard Base distribution information
commands.tooltip: Displays Linux Standard Base information about the distribution — similar to /etc/os-release but as a command.

commands.desc: Show disk space usage of all mounted filesystems in human-readable form
commands.tooltip: Shows how much disk space each mounted filesystem is using and how much is free. -h shows human-readable sizes.

commands.desc: Show disk usage with filesystem type included
commands.tooltip: Like df -h but also shows the filesystem type (ext4, ntfs, tmpfs, etc.) for each mounted drive.

commands.desc: Show inode usage of filesystems
commands.tooltip: Shows inode usage instead of disk space. Inodes are internal records for each file; running out can prevent new files even with free space.

commands.desc: Show total disk usage of a directory in human-readable form
commands.tooltip: Shows the total disk space used by a directory and all its contents. -s gives a summary total, -h makes it human-readable.

commands.desc: Show disk usage one level deep in a directory
commands.tooltip: Shows disk usage one level deep inside a directory — shows each immediate subdirectory's size without going deeper.

commands.desc: Find the largest files and dirs in a directory
commands.tooltip: Lists all files and directories sorted by size, largest first. Combines du, sort, and head to find space hogs.

commands.desc: Mount a device partition to a mount point
commands.tooltip: Attaches (mounts) a storage device partition to a directory so you can access its files.

commands.desc: Mount partition with explicit filesystem type
commands.tooltip: Mounts a partition and specifies the filesystem type (ext4, ntfs, etc.) explicitly instead of letting the system detect it.

commands.desc: Remount a filesystem as read-only
commands.tooltip: Remounts an already-mounted filesystem as read-only without unmounting it first. Useful for maintenance.

commands.desc: List all currently mounted filesystems
commands.tooltip: Lists all currently mounted filesystems, showing what device is mounted where and with what options.

commands.desc: Unmount a mounted filesystem
commands.tooltip: Detaches (unmounts) a mounted filesystem so it's no longer accessible and can be safely removed.

commands.desc: Lazy unmount — detach when not in use
commands.tooltip: Lazy unmount: marks the filesystem for unmounting but waits until no processes are using it before completing.

commands.desc: List all disk partitions and partition tables
commands.tooltip: Lists all disk partition tables — shows every partition on every disk, their sizes and types.

commands.desc: Start interactive partitioning of /dev/sdb
commands.tooltip: Opens an interactive partitioning tool for the disk /dev/sdb. Lets you create, delete, and resize partitions.

commands.desc: Display partition table using parted
commands.tooltip: Displays the current partition table of a disk using the parted tool.

commands.desc: Create a partition with parted
commands.tooltip: Creates a new partition on a disk using parted. Specifies the type, filesystem, and size range.

commands.desc: Format partition with ext4 filesystem
commands.tooltip: Formats a partition with the ext4 filesystem, preparing it for use on Linux. mkfs = 'make filesystem'.

commands.desc: Format partition with XFS filesystem
commands.tooltip: Formats a partition with XFS, a high-performance filesystem popular for large storage.

commands.desc: Format partition with FAT32 filesystem
commands.tooltip: Formats a partition with FAT32, which is compatible with Windows and commonly used for USB drives.

commands.desc: Set up a swap partition
commands.tooltip: Marks a partition as swap space — virtual memory that the system uses when RAM is full.

commands.desc: Enable a swap partition
commands.tooltip: Activates a swap partition so the system starts using it for virtual memory.

commands.desc: Disable a swap partition
commands.tooltip: Deactivates a swap partition, stopping the system from using it.

commands.desc: Check and repair a filesystem (run unmounted)
commands.tooltip: Checks a filesystem for errors and repairs them. Must be run on an unmounted filesystem.

commands.desc: Check and auto-repair filesystem answering yes to all
commands.tooltip: Checks and automatically repairs filesystem errors without asking for confirmation. -y answers 'yes' to everything.

commands.desc: Print block device attributes including UUID and filesystem type
commands.tooltip: Shows UUIDs (unique identifiers), filesystem types, and labels for all storage devices.

commands.desc: Show UUID and filesystem type of a specific partition
commands.tooltip: Shows the UUID and filesystem type for a specific partition.

commands.desc: Clone one entire disk to another
commands.tooltip: Copies an entire disk byte-for-byte to another disk. bs=64K sets the block size for efficient copying.

commands.desc: Create a disk image file from a device
commands.tooltip: Creates an image file (exact copy) of an entire disk. Useful for backups or cloning.

commands.desc: Create a 100MB file filled with zeros
commands.tooltip: Creates a file filled with zero bytes of a specific size. count=100 with bs=1M makes a 100 MB file.

commands.desc: Flush file system buffers — write cached data to disk
commands.tooltip: Forces all pending disk writes to actually be written to disk. Normally the system caches writes in RAM first.

commands.desc: Set a label on an ext2/3/4 filesystem
commands.tooltip: Sets a human-readable label on an ext4 filesystem — like giving the drive a name you can recognize.

commands.desc: List filesystem parameters and superblock info
commands.tooltip: Shows all parameters and settings of an ext filesystem — block size, UUID, creation date, mount count, etc.

commands.desc: Resize an ext2/3/4 filesystem to fill its partition
commands.tooltip: Resizes an ext filesystem to fill its entire partition (after you've resized the partition itself).

commands.desc: Resize an ext filesystem to a specific size
commands.tooltip: Resizes an ext filesystem to a specific size (20G = 20 gigabytes) rather than filling the partition.

commands.desc: Change the label on an ext filesystem
commands.tooltip: Changes the label (name) on an ext filesystem. The label appears when you mount or list the filesystem.

commands.desc: Force check of an ext filesystem
commands.tooltip: Forces a filesystem check even if it was cleanly unmounted. -f overrides the 'clean' flag.

commands.desc: Custom listing of block devices with specific columns
commands.tooltip: Shows block devices with only the columns you specify: name, size, type, and mount point.

commands.desc: Discard unused blocks on mounted SSD filesystems (TRIM)
commands.tooltip: Sends TRIM commands to SSDs for all mounted filesystems — frees blocks the SSD knows are unused, maintaining performance.

commands.desc: Get detailed hardware info about an ATA disk
commands.tooltip: Retrieves detailed technical information about an ATA/SATA drive, including model, firmware, and capabilities.

commands.desc: Display full SMART disk health information
commands.tooltip: Reads the SMART (Self-Monitoring, Analysis and Reporting Technology) health data from a drive to check for impending failures.

commands.desc: Refresh the package index from all configured repositories
commands.tooltip: Downloads the latest list of available packages and versions from the internet repositories. Like refreshing an app store. Run before upgrading.

commands.desc: Upgrade all installed packages to their latest versions
commands.tooltip: Installs the newest available versions of all currently installed packages.

commands.desc: Upgrade packages with dependency changes (may remove some)
commands.tooltip: Like apt upgrade but can also remove packages or install new dependencies that are required for upgrades.

commands.desc: Install a package
commands.tooltip: Downloads and installs the nginx package and all its dependencies.

commands.desc: Install package without interactive confirmation
commands.tooltip: Installs without asking 'are you sure?' — useful in scripts where you can't answer interactively.

commands.desc: Install package without recommended extras
commands.tooltip: Installs a package but skips the 'recommended' extras that would normally be installed alongside it.

commands.desc: Remove a package keeping configuration files
commands.tooltip: Removes a package but keeps its configuration files on disk — useful if you might reinstall it later.

commands.desc: Remove a package and delete its configuration files
commands.tooltip: Removes a package AND deletes its configuration files. Purge is more thorough than remove.

commands.desc: Remove automatically installed packages no longer needed
commands.tooltip: Removes packages that were automatically installed as dependencies but are no longer needed by anything.

commands.desc: Remove unneeded packages and their config files
commands.tooltip: Removes unneeded packages and also deletes their configuration files.

commands.desc: Search for packages matching a keyword
commands.tooltip: Searches the package list for packages whose name or description mentions the keyword.

commands.desc: Display detailed information about a package
commands.tooltip: Shows detailed information about a package: version, description, installed size, dependencies, etc.

commands.desc: List all installed packages
commands.tooltip: Lists all packages currently installed on the system.

commands.desc: List packages with available upgrades
commands.tooltip: Lists all installed packages for which a newer version is available.

commands.desc: Search package cache for a keyword
commands.tooltip: Searches the local package cache (apt-cache) for packages matching a keyword — doesn't require internet.

commands.desc: Show detailed info for a package from cache
commands.tooltip: Shows detailed package information from the local cache without going online.

commands.desc: Show installed and available versions of a package
commands.tooltip: Shows which version of a package is installed and which versions are available in the repositories.

commands.desc: Show package dependencies
commands.tooltip: Shows what other packages a given package depends on — its prerequisites.

commands.desc: Install a local .deb package file
commands.tooltip: Installs a .deb package file you've downloaded manually (not from a repository). dpkg is the lower-level package tool.

commands.desc: Remove an installed package via dpkg
commands.tooltip: Removes a package using the low-level dpkg tool directly.

commands.desc: List all installed packages via dpkg
commands.tooltip: Lists all packages installed via dpkg — a comprehensive list of everything on the system.

commands.desc: Check if a specific package is installed
commands.tooltip: Pipes the full package list through grep to check if a specific package is installed.

commands.desc: Find which package owns a file
commands.tooltip: Finds which package installed a specific file — useful for tracking down where a binary came from.

commands.desc: List all files installed by a package
commands.tooltip: Lists all the files that were installed by a specific package.

commands.desc: List all packages and their install state
commands.tooltip: Lists every package and whether it's installed, deinstalled, or purged.

commands.desc: Reconfigure an installed package interactively
commands.tooltip: Runs an interactive configuration wizard to reconfigure an already-installed package.

commands.desc: Add a PPA (Personal Package Archive) repository
commands.tooltip: Adds a PPA (Personal Package Archive) — a third-party package repository — to your package sources.

commands.desc: Remove a PPA repository
commands.tooltip: Removes a PPA from your package sources.

commands.desc: Install a snap package
commands.tooltip: Installs a snap package. Snap packages are self-contained apps that work across different Linux distributions.

commands.desc: Remove an installed snap package
commands.tooltip: Removes a snap package from the system.

commands.desc: List all installed snap packages
commands.tooltip: Lists all snap packages currently installed.

commands.desc: Update all installed snap packages
commands.tooltip: Updates all installed snap packages to their latest versions.

commands.desc: Search the snap store for packages
commands.tooltip: Searches the Snap Store for packages matching the keyword.

commands.desc: Install a Flatpak from Flathub
commands.tooltip: Installs a Flatpak application from the Flathub repository. Flatpak is a universal packaging format.

commands.desc: Uninstall a Flatpak application
commands.tooltip: Uninstalls a Flatpak application.

commands.desc: List all installed Flatpak applications
commands.tooltip: Lists all Flatpak applications currently installed.

commands.desc: Update all installed Flatpak applications
commands.tooltip: Updates all installed Flatpak applications.

commands.desc: Hold a package at its current version (no upgrades)
commands.tooltip: Prevents a package from being upgraded during apt upgrade. 'Hold' pins it at its current version.

commands.desc: Remove the hold and allow upgrades again
commands.tooltip: Removes the hold on a package, allowing it to be upgraded again.

commands.desc: Clear the local package cache to free disk space
commands.tooltip: Deletes all downloaded package files from the local cache to free up disk space.

commands.desc: Remove outdated package files from the local cache
commands.tooltip: Removes only outdated package files from the cache — keeps the current version but removes old ones.

commands.desc: Send ICMP echo requests to test connectivity
commands.tooltip: Sends small test packets to a host and measures how long they take to come back. Used to check if a server is reachable.

commands.desc: Send exactly 4 ping packets then stop
commands.tooltip: Sends exactly 4 ping packets then stops. Without -c, ping runs forever until you press Ctrl+C.

commands.desc: Ping with a 0.5-second interval between packets
commands.tooltip: Pings with a 0.5-second gap between packets instead of the default 1 second.

commands.desc: Ping using IPv6
commands.tooltip: Pings using IPv6 — the newer Internet Protocol addressing format.

commands.desc: Trace the network path to a destination host
commands.tooltip: Shows the route (list of network hops/routers) that packets take to reach a destination.

commands.desc: Trace route showing IP addresses instead of hostnames
commands.tooltip: Shows traceroute output with IP addresses instead of trying to resolve each hop to a hostname. Faster.

commands.desc: Combined traceroute and ping — live network diagnostics
commands.tooltip: A live, continuously updated tool that combines traceroute and ping — shows both route and packet loss in real time.

commands.desc: Run mtr and generate a static report
commands.tooltip: Runs mtr and outputs a single static report instead of live updates — useful for sharing results.

commands.desc: Show all network interfaces and their IP addresses
commands.tooltip: Shows all network interfaces (like eth0 for wired, wlan0 for wireless) and their IP addresses.

commands.desc: Show IP address info for a specific interface
commands.tooltip: Shows IP address details for a specific network interface only.

commands.desc: Assign an IP address to an interface
commands.tooltip: Assigns an IP address to a network interface. The /24 specifies the subnet mask (255.255.255.0).

commands.desc: Remove an IP address from an interface
commands.tooltip: Removes an IP address from a network interface.

commands.desc: Show the routing table
commands.tooltip: Shows the routing table — the rules the system uses to decide where to send each packet.

commands.desc: Add a default gateway route
commands.tooltip: Adds a default gateway — the router that handles all traffic not matching a more specific route.

commands.desc: Add a static route
commands.tooltip: Adds a specific route so traffic to 10.0.0.0/8 goes through the specified gateway.

commands.desc: Show all network interfaces and their state
commands.tooltip: Shows all network interfaces and whether they're up or down.

commands.desc: Bring a network interface up
commands.tooltip: Brings a network interface online (activates it).

commands.desc: Bring a network interface down
commands.tooltip: Takes a network interface offline (deactivates it).

commands.desc: Display or configure network interfaces (legacy tool)
commands.tooltip: An older tool for displaying and configuring network interfaces. Now superseded by 'ip' commands.

commands.desc: Bring up network interface eth0
commands.tooltip: Activates the eth0 network interface using the legacy ifconfig tool.

commands.desc: List all listening TCP/UDP sockets with ports
commands.tooltip: Lists all listening ports (where a service is waiting for connections). -t=TCP, -u=UDP, -l=listening, -n=numbers not names.

commands.desc: List listening sockets showing the process name and PID
commands.tooltip: Like ss -tuln but also shows the program name and PID that owns each socket.

commands.desc: Show a summary of socket statistics
commands.tooltip: Shows a statistical summary of sockets: total counts by state (established, listening, etc.).

commands.desc: List all listening ports (legacy, prefer ss)
commands.tooltip: Lists listening ports using the older netstat tool. ss is the modern replacement.

commands.desc: Show the routing table
commands.tooltip: Shows the routing table using netstat. 'ip route' is the modern equivalent.

commands.desc: Show network interface statistics
commands.tooltip: Shows per-interface network statistics: packets sent/received, errors, collisions.

commands.desc: Scan a host for open ports
commands.tooltip: Scans a host for open ports, telling you which services are accepting connections.

commands.desc: Detect service versions on open ports
commands.tooltip: Detects which software version is running on each open port of a host. -sV means 'service version detection'.

commands.desc: Scan specific ports on a host
commands.tooltip: Scans only the specified ports (80 for HTTP, 443 for HTTPS) instead of all ports.

commands.desc: Ping scan to discover live hosts in a subnet
commands.tooltip: Discovers which hosts on a network are online without doing a full port scan. -sn means 'ping scan only'.

commands.desc: Make an HTTP GET request and print response body
commands.tooltip: Makes an HTTP GET request to a URL and prints the response. Like a browser that shows raw content.

commands.desc: Fetch only HTTP headers from a URL
commands.tooltip: Downloads and shows only the HTTP response headers (metadata), not the body content.

commands.desc: Follow redirects automatically
commands.tooltip: Follows HTTP redirects automatically. Without -L, curl stops at the redirect and doesn't follow it.

commands.desc: Download URL content to a file
commands.tooltip: Downloads a URL's content and saves it to a file. -o specifies the filename.

commands.desc: Send an HTTP POST request with form data
commands.tooltip: Sends form data to a URL using HTTP POST — the method used for submitting forms or sending data to APIs.

commands.desc: Add a custom header to a request
commands.tooltip: Adds a custom HTTP header to the request — commonly used for authentication tokens.

commands.desc: HTTP basic authentication with curl
commands.tooltip: Sends a username and password using HTTP Basic Authentication.

commands.desc: Skip SSL certificate verification
commands.tooltip: Disables SSL certificate verification — useful for testing servers with self-signed certificates. -k means 'insecure'.

commands.desc: Set a 10-second timeout for a request
commands.tooltip: Sets a maximum time limit for the request. If the server doesn't respond within 10 seconds, curl gives up.

commands.desc: Download a file from the web
commands.tooltip: Downloads a file from the internet. simpler to use than curl for basic file downloads.

commands.desc: Download and save with a specific filename
commands.tooltip: Downloads a file and saves it with a specific filename instead of using the remote filename.

commands.desc: Continue/resume a partially downloaded file
commands.tooltip: Resumes an interrupted download instead of starting over. Useful for large files or unstable connections.

commands.desc: Download quietly with no output
commands.tooltip: Downloads silently — no progress bar or status messages. -q means 'quiet'.

commands.desc: Mirror an entire website recursively
commands.tooltip: Downloads an entire website recursively — all pages and linked resources. Use cautiously.

commands.desc: Test if port 80 is open on a host (TCP check)
commands.tooltip: Tests if a specific port is open on a host without connecting to a service. -z means 'zero I/O mode' (just test), -v means 'verbose'.

commands.desc: Listen on port 8080 (simple netcat server)
commands.tooltip: Starts netcat as a simple server listening for incoming connections on port 8080.

commands.desc: Send a file to a remote netcat listener
commands.tooltip: Sends the contents of a file to a remote netcat listener — a basic way to transfer data.

commands.desc: Connect to a port using telnet (test SMTP, etc.)
commands.tooltip: Connects to a specific port on a host using the telnet protocol — useful for manually testing servers like email (port 25).

commands.desc: Perform a DNS lookup for a domain
commands.tooltip: Looks up the IP address and DNS records for a domain name. dig stands for 'Domain Information Groper'.

commands.desc: Look up mail exchange (MX) DNS records
commands.tooltip: Looks up MX (Mail Exchange) records — the servers responsible for receiving email for a domain.

commands.desc: Show only the IP address result of a DNS lookup
commands.tooltip: Shows only the answer (IP address or record value) without all the extra DNS response information.

commands.desc: Query a specific DNS server
commands.tooltip: Queries a specific DNS server (like Google's 8.8.8.8) instead of your system's default.

commands.desc: Query DNS for a domain name (legacy tool)
commands.tooltip: A simpler DNS lookup tool. Shows the IP address for a hostname. Older than dig.

commands.desc: Simple DNS lookup utility
commands.tooltip: A clean, simple DNS lookup utility that shows the IP for a hostname or the hostname for an IP.

commands.desc: Look up domain registration and WHOIS info
commands.tooltip: Looks up domain registration information: who owns the domain, when it expires, and the nameservers.

commands.desc: Display the ARP cache (IP to MAC mappings)
commands.tooltip: Shows the ARP (Address Resolution Protocol) cache — a table of IP addresses and their corresponding physical MAC addresses on your local network.

commands.desc: Display the routing table in numeric format
commands.tooltip: Shows the routing table using the legacy route command. 'ip route' is the modern equivalent.

commands.desc: Display or change Ethernet interface settings
commands.tooltip: Shows technical details about an Ethernet interface: link speed, duplex mode, driver information.

commands.desc: Show status of all network devices via NetworkManager
commands.tooltip: Shows the status of all network interfaces managed by NetworkManager — whether they're connected or disconnected.

commands.desc: List all NetworkManager connections
commands.tooltip: Lists all saved network connection profiles managed by NetworkManager.

commands.desc: Connect to a NetworkManager connection profile
commands.tooltip: Connects to a saved NetworkManager profile (like a WiFi network you've used before).

commands.desc: Capture packets on interface eth0
commands.tooltip: Starts capturing all network packets on interface eth0 and prints them to the terminal.

commands.desc: Capture only packets on port 80
commands.tooltip: Captures only packets on port 80 (HTTP web traffic) instead of all traffic.

commands.desc: Write packet capture to a file
commands.tooltip: Saves captured packets to a file for later analysis with tools like Wireshark.

commands.desc: Connect to remote host as a specific user via SSH
commands.tooltip: Connects to a remote computer over the network via SSH (Secure Shell) — an encrypted terminal session.

commands.desc: Connect via SSH on a non-standard port
commands.tooltip: Connects via SSH on a non-standard port. The default SSH port is 22; servers sometimes use different ports for security.

commands.desc: Connect using a specific private key file
commands.tooltip: Connects using a specific private key file for authentication instead of a password. The private key proves your identity.

commands.desc: Connect with verbose output (debug connection issues)
commands.tooltip: Connects with verbose debug output — prints detailed information about the connection process, useful for diagnosing SSH failures.

commands.desc: Connect with SSH agent forwarding enabled
commands.tooltip: Enables agent forwarding: lets the remote server use your local SSH keys to connect to other servers. Useful for multi-hop SSH.

commands.desc: Connect with X11 forwarding (remote GUI apps)
commands.tooltip: Enables X11 forwarding so graphical applications on the remote server can display on your local screen.

commands.desc: Local port forwarding: tunnel local 8080 to remote port 80
commands.tooltip: Creates a secure tunnel: traffic to your local port 8080 is forwarded through the SSH connection to port 80 on the remote server.

commands.desc: Remote port forwarding: expose local port 80 via remote host
commands.tooltip: Reverse tunnel: makes your local service (port 80) accessible via the remote server's port 8080.

commands.desc: Create a SOCKS5 proxy via SSH dynamic port forwarding
commands.tooltip: Creates a SOCKS5 proxy on your local port 1080. You can configure your browser to use this as a proxy server.

commands.desc: SSH through a jump/bastion host to reach target
commands.tooltip: Connects to a target server by bouncing through an intermediate jump host (also called a bastion host).

commands.desc: Generate a 4096-bit RSA SSH key pair
commands.tooltip: Generates a new SSH key pair for authentication. RSA 4096-bit is secure. The comment (-C) is usually your email.

commands.desc: Generate a modern Ed25519 SSH key pair
commands.tooltip: Generates an Ed25519 key pair — a modern, smaller, and faster alternative to RSA that's equally secure.

commands.desc: Generate a key pair with a specific filename
commands.tooltip: Generates an SSH key pair and saves it with a custom filename instead of the default id_rsa.

commands.desc: Change the passphrase of an existing key
commands.tooltip: Changes the passphrase protecting an existing private key without changing the key itself.

commands.desc: Remove a host's entry from known_hosts file
commands.tooltip: Removes a host's entry from your known_hosts file — necessary when a server's key changes to avoid a warning.

commands.desc: Copy default public key to remote host's authorized_keys
commands.tooltip: Copies your default public key to the remote server's authorized_keys file, enabling password-free login.

commands.desc: Copy a specific public key to remote host
commands.tooltip: Copies a specific public key to a remote server's authorized_keys file.

commands.desc: Start the SSH agent and launch a new bash shell
commands.tooltip: Starts the SSH agent (which holds your decrypted keys in memory) and launches a new bash shell.

commands.desc: Start SSH agent and export environment variables
commands.tooltip: Starts the SSH agent and exports its address into environment variables so other commands can find it.

commands.desc: Add an SSH private key to the authentication agent
commands.tooltip: Loads a private key into the SSH agent. After this, you won't need to enter the key's passphrase for each connection.

commands.desc: List fingerprints of keys loaded in ssh-agent
commands.tooltip: Lists the fingerprints of all SSH keys currently loaded in the agent.

commands.desc: Copy a local file to a remote host
commands.tooltip: Copies a local file to a remote server using SCP (Secure Copy Protocol), which uses SSH for encryption.

commands.desc: Copy a file from remote host to local directory
commands.tooltip: Downloads a file from a remote server to the current local directory (the . means 'here').

commands.desc: Copy an entire directory to a remote host
commands.tooltip: Copies an entire directory to a remote server. -r means 'recursive'.

commands.desc: Copy to remote host on a non-default port
commands.tooltip: Copies to a remote server on a non-default port. Note: SCP uses -P (capital) unlike SSH which uses -p.

commands.desc: Start an interactive SFTP session to a remote host
commands.tooltip: Starts an interactive SFTP (SSH File Transfer Protocol) session — like FTP but encrypted.

commands.desc: Start SFTP session on a non-standard port
commands.tooltip: Starts an SFTP session connecting to a server on a non-standard port.

commands.desc: Sync files to remote host over SSH with compression
commands.tooltip: Syncs files to a remote server over SSH with compression (-z). Combines rsync's efficiency with SSH's security.

commands.desc: View SSH client configuration file with host aliases
commands.tooltip: Shows your SSH client config file where you can define aliases and settings for hosts you connect to often.

commands.desc: View public keys authorized to access this account
commands.tooltip: Shows the list of SSH public keys that are authorized to log into your account without a password.

commands.desc: Create a gzip-compressed tarball from a directory
commands.tooltip: Creates a compressed archive (tarball) of a directory. -c=create, -z=gzip compress, -f=filename. The .tar.gz extension is standard.

commands.desc: Create a bzip2-compressed tarball
commands.tooltip: Creates a bzip2-compressed tarball. bzip2 usually compresses better than gzip but is slower.

commands.desc: Create an xz-compressed tarball
commands.tooltip: Creates an xz-compressed tarball. xz has the best compression ratio but is the slowest.

commands.desc: Create an uncompressed tarball
commands.tooltip: Creates an uncompressed tarball — just bundles files together without compressing them.

commands.desc: Extract a gzip-compressed tarball
commands.tooltip: Extracts a gzip-compressed tarball. -x=extract, -z=gzip, -f=filename.

commands.desc: Extract a bzip2-compressed tarball
commands.tooltip: Extracts a bzip2-compressed tarball.

commands.desc: Extract an xz-compressed tarball
commands.tooltip: Extracts an xz-compressed tarball.

commands.desc: Extract tarball to a specific directory
commands.tooltip: Extracts a tarball's contents into a specific directory (-C specifies where).

commands.desc: List the contents of a gzip tarball
commands.tooltip: Lists the contents of a gzip tarball without extracting — shows what's inside.

commands.desc: Append a file to an existing tar archive
commands.tooltip: Adds a new file to an existing tar archive. -r means 'append'.

commands.desc: Delete a file from a tar archive
commands.tooltip: Removes a specific file from inside an existing tar archive.

commands.desc: Stream tar archive over SSH to remote host
commands.tooltip: Creates a tarball and streams it directly over SSH to save it on a remote server without creating a local file.

commands.desc: Compress a file with gzip (creates file.txt.gz)
commands.tooltip: Compresses a file using gzip. The original is replaced with a .gz file. gzip is the most common Unix compression tool.

commands.desc: Decompress a gzip file
commands.tooltip: Decompresses a gzip file. -d means 'decompress'.

commands.desc: Compress keeping the original file
commands.tooltip: Compresses a file with gzip but also keeps the original uncompressed file. -k means 'keep'.

commands.desc: Compress with maximum compression level
commands.tooltip: Compresses with maximum compression effort (-9). Produces the smallest file but takes longer.

commands.desc: Decompress a .gz file (same as gzip -d)
commands.tooltip: Decompresses a .gz file. Equivalent to gzip -d.

commands.desc: Display contents of a gzip file without extracting
commands.tooltip: Shows the contents of a gzip-compressed file without extracting it to disk.

commands.desc: Page through a gzip compressed file
commands.tooltip: Opens a gzip-compressed file in the less pager so you can scroll through it without extracting.

commands.desc: Compress a file with bzip2 (better ratio than gzip)
commands.tooltip: Compresses a file using bzip2 — typically achieves better compression than gzip.

commands.desc: Decompress a bzip2 file
commands.tooltip: Decompresses a bzip2-compressed file.

commands.desc: Compress with bzip2 keeping the original
commands.tooltip: Compresses with bzip2 while keeping the original file.

commands.desc: Compress a file with xz (excellent compression ratio)
commands.tooltip: Compresses a file with xz — achieves the best compression ratio among common tools, at the cost of speed.

commands.desc: Decompress an xz file
commands.tooltip: Decompresses an xz-compressed file.

commands.desc: Compress with xz keeping the original file
commands.tooltip: Compresses with xz while keeping the original file.

commands.desc: Create a zip archive containing files
commands.tooltip: Creates a zip archive containing the listed files. Zip is universal — works on Windows, Mac, and Linux.

commands.desc: Create a zip archive of a directory recursively
commands.tooltip: Creates a zip archive of an entire directory and all its contents. -r means 'recursive'.

commands.desc: Create an encrypted zip archive
commands.tooltip: Creates an encrypted, password-protected zip archive. -e prompts for a password.

commands.desc: Extract a zip archive
commands.tooltip: Extracts all files from a zip archive.

commands.desc: Extract zip to a specific directory
commands.tooltip: Extracts a zip archive's files into a specific target directory.

commands.desc: List contents of a zip archive without extracting
commands.tooltip: Shows what's inside a zip archive without extracting it.

commands.desc: Create a 7-Zip archive
commands.tooltip: Creates a 7-Zip archive (.7z) — excellent compression, popular for large files. Requires p7zip.

commands.desc: Extract a 7-Zip archive
commands.tooltip: Extracts a 7-Zip archive. -x means 'extract with full paths'.

commands.desc: List contents of a 7-Zip archive
commands.tooltip: Lists the contents of a 7-Zip archive.

commands.desc: Create a RAR archive
commands.tooltip: Creates a RAR archive. RAR is a proprietary format common on Windows. Requires rar to be installed.

commands.desc: Extract a RAR archive
commands.tooltip: Extracts a RAR archive with full directory paths. Requires unrar to be installed.

commands.desc: Start a service immediately
commands.tooltip: Starts a service (background program) right now. systemctl is the control tool for systemd, the modern Linux service manager.

commands.desc: Stop a running service
commands.tooltip: Stops a running service immediately.

commands.desc: Stop and start a service
commands.tooltip: Stops and then immediately restarts a service — useful for applying configuration changes.

commands.desc: Reload service configuration without full restart
commands.tooltip: Tells a service to re-read its configuration files without fully restarting — faster and less disruptive.

commands.desc: Show the current status of a service
commands.tooltip: Shows whether a service is running, stopped, or failed, along with recent log entries.

commands.desc: Enable a service to start automatically at boot
commands.tooltip: Configures a service to start automatically every time the system boots.

commands.desc: Disable a service from starting at boot
commands.tooltip: Prevents a service from starting automatically at boot. It can still be started manually.

commands.desc: Enable and immediately start a service
commands.tooltip: Enables a service at boot AND starts it right now in one command.

commands.desc: Disable and immediately stop a service
commands.tooltip: Disables boot startup AND stops the service right now in one command.

commands.desc: Completely prevent a service from being started
commands.tooltip: Completely prevents a service from being started by anyone, even root. More restrictive than disable.

commands.desc: Remove mask so service can be started again
commands.tooltip: Removes the mask so a previously blocked service can be started again.

commands.desc: Reload systemd manager configuration after unit changes
commands.tooltip: Reloads systemd's configuration after you've added or modified a service unit file. Always run this after changing unit files.

commands.desc: List all active systemd units
commands.tooltip: Lists all active systemd units — services, timers, sockets, mounts, etc.

commands.desc: List only service units
commands.tooltip: Lists only service units, filtering out the other types.

commands.desc: List units that have failed
commands.tooltip: Shows only units that are in a failed state — useful for diagnosing problems.

commands.desc: List all installed unit files and their enable state
commands.tooltip: Lists all installed unit files and shows whether each is enabled, disabled, or masked.

commands.desc: List all active systemd timer units
commands.tooltip: Lists all active timer units — systemd's modern alternative to cron for scheduled tasks.

commands.desc: Check if a service is currently active
commands.tooltip: Checks if a service is currently running. Exits with code 0 (success) if active, 1 if not.

commands.desc: Check if a service is enabled at boot
commands.tooltip: Checks if a service is configured to start at boot. Exits 0 if enabled, 1 if disabled.

commands.desc: Check if a service is in a failed state
commands.tooltip: Checks if a service is in a failed state — useful in scripts for automated health monitoring.

commands.desc: Switch to multi-user mode (like runlevel 3)
commands.tooltip: Switches the system to multi-user (non-graphical) mode, similar to old-school runlevel 3.

commands.desc: Show the default boot target
commands.tooltip: Shows what the system boots into by default (e.g. graphical.target or multi-user.target).

commands.desc: Set the default boot target to graphical
commands.tooltip: Sets the default boot mode to graphical (with a desktop environment).

commands.desc: Print the content of a service's unit file
commands.tooltip: Shows the contents of the service's unit file — the configuration that defines how the service runs.

commands.desc: Edit or create a drop-in override for a service
commands.tooltip: Opens an editor to add custom overrides to a service's unit file without modifying the original.

commands.desc: Reboot the system via systemd
commands.tooltip: Reboots the system gracefully through systemd.

commands.desc: Power off the system via systemd
commands.tooltip: Powers off the system gracefully through systemd.

commands.desc: Suspend the system to RAM
commands.tooltip: Suspends the system to RAM (sleep mode), saving power while keeping your session in memory.

commands.desc: View all logs from the systemd journal
commands.tooltip: Shows all stored log messages from systemd's journal. Press q to quit, use arrow keys to scroll.

commands.desc: View journal logs for a specific service
commands.tooltip: Shows journal logs for a specific service only, filtering out all other messages.

commands.desc: Follow the journal log in real-time (like tail -f)
commands.tooltip: Follows the journal log in real time, printing new entries as they arrive. Like tail -f for system logs.

commands.desc: Show the last 50 journal log entries
commands.tooltip: Shows only the most recent 50 log entries instead of the entire log history.

commands.desc: Show journal entries from the last hour
commands.tooltip: Shows only log entries from the last hour — useful for recent troubleshooting.

commands.desc: Show journal entries in a date range
commands.tooltip: Shows log entries within a specific date range — useful for investigating an incident.

commands.desc: Show only error-level and higher journal entries
commands.tooltip: Shows only error-level and higher messages (error, critical, alert, emergency), filtering out info and warnings.

commands.desc: Show how much disk space the journal is using
commands.tooltip: Shows how much disk space the systemd journal is currently using for stored logs.

commands.desc: Reduce journal size to 100MB by removing old entries
commands.tooltip: Trims old journal entries to bring disk usage down to 100MB.

commands.desc: Show journal entries from the current boot
commands.tooltip: Shows log entries only from the current boot session, filtering out all previous boots.

commands.desc: Show journal entries from the previous boot
commands.tooltip: Shows log entries from the boot session before the current one — useful for investigating problems before a reboot.

commands.desc: Restart a service using the legacy service command
commands.tooltip: Restarts a service using the older SysV-style service command. Most systems now prefer 'systemctl restart'.

commands.desc: Edit the current user's crontab file in default editor
commands.tooltip: Opens your personal cron schedule file in a text editor, letting you add automated tasks. Cron runs commands on a schedule.

commands.desc: List the current user's scheduled cron jobs
commands.tooltip: Shows all your currently scheduled cron jobs.

commands.desc: Remove the current user's entire crontab
commands.tooltip: Deletes your entire crontab (schedule). Use with caution — this removes all your scheduled jobs.

commands.desc: Edit the crontab for user alice (as root)
commands.tooltip: Edits the crontab for a specific user (run as root to manage other users' schedules).

commands.desc: List alice's cron jobs
commands.tooltip: Lists alice's scheduled cron jobs (as root).

commands.desc: Cron syntax: run command every minute
commands.tooltip: The five * * * * * fields mean: minute, hour, day-of-month, month, day-of-week. All asterisks = every minute.

commands.desc: Cron syntax: run command at the top of every hour
commands.tooltip: Runs a command at the start of every hour. The 0 in the minute position means 'at minute zero'.

commands.desc: Cron syntax: run command daily at 2:00 AM
commands.tooltip: Runs a command every day at 2:00 AM. Common for backups and maintenance tasks.

commands.desc: Cron syntax: run command every Sunday at 2:00 AM
commands.tooltip: Runs a command every Sunday at 2:00 AM. Day 0 (and 7) = Sunday in cron.

commands.desc: Cron syntax: run command on the 1st of each month at 2 AM
commands.tooltip: Runs a command on the 1st day of every month at 2 AM.

commands.desc: Cron syntax: run command once at system startup
commands.tooltip: @reboot is a special shorthand that runs the command once immediately after the system starts.

commands.desc: Cron shorthand: run command once per day
commands.tooltip: @daily is shorthand for '0 0 * * *' — runs once per day at midnight.

commands.desc: Cron shorthand: run command once per week
commands.tooltip: @weekly is shorthand for '0 0 * * 0' — runs once per week on Sunday at midnight.

commands.desc: Cron syntax: run command every 5 minutes
commands.tooltip: */5 means 'every 5 minutes' — the / with a number creates a step interval.

commands.desc: Cron: run every hour from 9-5 on weekdays only
commands.tooltip: Runs every hour on the hour but only between 9 AM and 5 PM (9-17) and only on weekdays (1-5, Monday through Friday).

commands.desc: Schedule a one-time command to run at 2:30 PM
commands.tooltip: Schedules a one-time command to run at a specific time. The 'at' command is for one-off scheduled tasks.

commands.desc: Schedule a command to run 1 hour from now
commands.tooltip: Schedules a command to run 1 hour from the current time.

commands.desc: Schedule a command to run at midnight
commands.tooltip: Schedules a command to run at midnight tonight.

commands.desc: List all pending at jobs
commands.tooltip: Lists all pending jobs scheduled with the 'at' command.

commands.desc: Remove at job number 5
commands.tooltip: Cancels a specific at job. Use 'atq' to see job numbers.

commands.desc: List system cron jobs in the /etc/cron.d directory
commands.tooltip: Lists system-level cron job files stored in /etc/cron.d/ — these run for the whole system, not per-user.

commands.desc: View the system-wide crontab file
commands.tooltip: Shows the system-wide crontab, which runs jobs with specific users specified in each line.

commands.desc: List scripts that run daily via run-parts
commands.tooltip: Lists scripts that are automatically run daily by the run-parts mechanism.

commands.desc: List scripts that run every hour
commands.tooltip: Lists scripts that are automatically run every hour by the run-parts mechanism.

commands.desc: Manually run all scripts in the daily cron directory
commands.tooltip: Manually triggers all scripts in the daily cron directory right now, without waiting for the scheduled time.

commands.desc: Run a command when system load drops below threshold
commands.tooltip: Queues a command to run when the system's CPU load average falls below a threshold, for low-priority work.

commands.desc: Force run all anacron jobs immediately
commands.tooltip: Forces all anacron jobs to run immediately. anacron handles tasks on systems that aren't always on.

commands.desc: Check syslog for cron job execution history
commands.tooltip: Filters the system log for CRON-related entries to see a history of cron job executions.

commands.desc: List systemd timers as a modern cron alternative
commands.tooltip: Lists systemd timers — the modern replacement for cron. Shows when each timer last ran and when it will next run.

commands.desc: Run cron job with file lock to prevent parallel runs
commands.tooltip: Runs a cron job with a file lock: if the previous run is still going, this new run is skipped. Prevents overlapping jobs.

commands.desc: Initialize a new empty Git repository in current directory
commands.tooltip: Creates a new, empty Git repository in the current directory. Git tracks changes to your files over time.

commands.desc: Initialize a new Git repository in a named directory
commands.tooltip: Creates a new Git repository in a new directory with the given name.

commands.desc: Clone a remote repository to a local directory
commands.tooltip: Downloads a complete copy of a remote repository to your local machine, including all history.

commands.desc: Shallow clone with only the latest commit history
commands.tooltip: Downloads only the most recent snapshot of a repository, not the full history. Faster and uses less disk space. --depth 1 means 'only the latest commit'.

commands.desc: Clone a specific branch only
commands.tooltip: Clones only a specific branch from the repository instead of all branches.

commands.desc: Show the working tree status (modified, staged, untracked files)
commands.tooltip: Shows which files have been modified, added, or deleted, and whether they're staged (ready to commit) or not.

commands.desc: Show status in short format
commands.tooltip: Shows status in a compact abbreviated format — one line per file.

commands.desc: Stage a specific file for commit
commands.tooltip: Stages a specific file for the next commit. 'Staging' means marking it as ready to be saved (committed).

commands.desc: Stage all changes in the current directory
commands.tooltip: Stages all changes in the current directory and below — new files, modifications, and deletions.

commands.desc: Stage all changes including deletions across the whole repo
commands.tooltip: Stages all changes across the entire repository, including deletions, regardless of your current location.

commands.desc: Interactively select hunks of changes to stage
commands.tooltip: Lets you interactively choose which parts (hunks) of changed files to stage. -p means 'patch mode'.

commands.desc: Stage modifications and deletions but not new files
commands.tooltip: Stages only modifications and deletions to files already tracked by Git, but not brand-new untracked files.

commands.desc: Commit staged changes with a message
commands.tooltip: Saves all staged changes as a permanent snapshot (commit) with a descriptive message. The commit message should explain what changed.

commands.desc: Stage tracked files and commit in one step
commands.tooltip: Stages all tracked modified files and commits them in a single step. Does NOT include new untracked files.

commands.desc: Amend the most recent commit (message or content)
commands.tooltip: Opens an editor to modify the most recent commit — change its message or add more staged changes.

commands.desc: Add staged changes to last commit without editing message
commands.tooltip: Adds any currently staged changes to the last commit without changing the commit message.

commands.desc: Create a commit with no file changes
commands.tooltip: Creates a commit with no actual file changes. Useful for triggering CI pipelines.

commands.desc: Push local main branch to remote named origin
commands.tooltip: Uploads your local branch commits to the 'origin' remote (the server, usually GitHub). main is the branch name.

commands.desc: Push and set upstream tracking for the branch
commands.tooltip: Pushes and also sets up tracking: future 'git push' and 'git pull' commands will automatically use this branch.

commands.desc: Force push safely — fails if remote has new commits
commands.tooltip: Force-pushes (overwrites remote history) but safely — it fails if someone else has pushed since your last fetch.

commands.desc: Delete a remote branch
commands.tooltip: Deletes a branch on the remote server (GitHub, etc.).

commands.desc: Push all local tags to the remote repository
commands.tooltip: Uploads all local tags to the remote repository.

commands.desc: Fetch and merge changes from the tracked upstream branch
commands.tooltip: Downloads new commits from the remote and merges them into your current branch.

commands.desc: Fetch and rebase local commits on top of upstream changes
commands.tooltip: Downloads new commits and replays your local commits on top of them. Keeps a cleaner, linear history.

commands.desc: Pull from a specific remote and branch
commands.tooltip: Pulls from a specific remote (origin) and branch (main) explicitly.

commands.desc: Download changes from remote without merging
commands.tooltip: Downloads changes from the remote but does NOT merge them — lets you inspect before merging.

commands.desc: Fetch all changes from the origin remote
commands.tooltip: Fetches all changes from the 'origin' remote specifically.

commands.desc: Fetch from all configured remotes
commands.tooltip: Fetches from all configured remotes at once.

commands.desc: Fetch and remove remote-tracking branches no longer on remote
commands.tooltip: Fetches and removes any local tracking branches that no longer exist on the remote.

commands.desc: List local branches (current branch highlighted)
commands.tooltip: Lists all local branches. The current branch is marked with an asterisk (*).

commands.desc: List all local and remote branches
commands.tooltip: Lists both local branches and remote-tracking branches. -a means 'all'.

commands.desc: List remote-tracking branches
commands.tooltip: Lists only remote-tracking branches — copies of branches on the remote server.

commands.desc: Create a new local branch
commands.tooltip: Creates a new local branch. You stay on your current branch; use checkout or switch to move to it.

commands.desc: Rename a local branch
commands.tooltip: Renames a local branch. -m means 'move/rename'.

commands.desc: Delete a fully merged local branch
commands.tooltip: Deletes a local branch that has already been merged. Safe — it won't delete unmerged work.

commands.desc: Force delete a local branch regardless of merge status
commands.tooltip: Force-deletes a local branch even if it has unmerged commits. -D means 'force delete'. Use with caution.

commands.desc: Switch to an existing branch
commands.tooltip: Switches to an existing branch (the older git syntax). HEAD moves to that branch.

commands.desc: Create and switch to a new branch in one step
commands.tooltip: Creates a new branch and switches to it in one step.

commands.desc: Discard unstaged changes in a file (restore from HEAD)
commands.tooltip: Discards all unsaved changes to a file, restoring it to how it looked in the last commit (HEAD).

commands.desc: Switch to an existing branch (modern syntax)
commands.tooltip: Switches to an existing branch using the newer git switch syntax (cleaner than checkout for branch switching).

commands.desc: Create and switch to a new branch (modern syntax)
commands.tooltip: Creates and switches to a new branch using the modern syntax. -c means 'create'.

commands.desc: Discard working directory changes in a file
commands.tooltip: Discards working directory changes to a file, restoring it from the last commit.

commands.desc: Unstage a file while keeping working directory changes
commands.tooltip: Removes a file from the staging area (unstages it) without losing your changes in the file.

commands.desc: Merge a branch into the current branch
commands.tooltip: Merges another branch into the current one, combining their histories.

commands.desc: Merge creating a merge commit even if fast-forward possible
commands.tooltip: Forces a merge commit to be created even when a fast-forward would work — preserves the branch history.

commands.desc: Squash all branch commits into a single staged change
commands.tooltip: Combines all commits from the feature branch into a single uncommitted change, ready for you to commit as one.

commands.desc: Abort an in-progress merge and restore pre-merge state
commands.tooltip: Cancels a merge that's in progress and returns the repository to the state before the merge started.

commands.desc: Rebase current branch on top of main
commands.tooltip: Moves your current branch's commits to start from the tip of the main branch, as if you branched off from there.

commands.desc: Interactive rebase for the last 3 commits
commands.tooltip: Opens an interactive editor to rewrite the last 3 commits — reorder, combine (squash), or edit their messages.

commands.desc: Rebase commits between base and feature onto main
commands.tooltip: Rebases only the commits between 'base' and 'feature' onto 'main' — useful for transplanting a sub-range of commits.

commands.desc: Abort an in-progress rebase
commands.tooltip: Cancels an in-progress rebase and returns to the state before it started.

commands.desc: Continue a rebase after resolving conflicts
commands.tooltip: Resumes a paused rebase after you've resolved a conflict.

commands.desc: Temporarily save uncommitted changes to the stash
commands.tooltip: Saves all your uncommitted changes to a temporary stash so you can switch branches with a clean working directory.

commands.desc: Stash with a descriptive message
commands.tooltip: Stashes changes with a descriptive label so you can identify this stash later.

commands.desc: Apply the most recent stash and remove it from the stack
commands.tooltip: Applies the most recent stash entry to the working directory and removes it from the stash list.

commands.desc: Apply a specific stash entry without removing it
commands.tooltip: Applies a specific stash entry (stash@{2}) to the working directory but keeps it in the stash list.

commands.desc: List all stashed changes
commands.tooltip: Shows all entries in the stash with their labels and indices.

commands.desc: Delete a specific stash entry
commands.tooltip: Permanently deletes a specific stash entry without applying it.

commands.desc: Remove all stash entries
commands.tooltip: Deletes all stash entries at once.

commands.desc: Create a branch from a stash entry
commands.tooltip: Creates a new branch starting from the state captured in a stash entry — useful for resuming stashed work in a fresh context.

commands.desc: Show the commit log with author, date, and message
commands.tooltip: Shows the history of commits: who made each change, when, and with what message.

commands.desc: Show compact commit log, one line per commit
commands.tooltip: Shows commit history compactly — one line per commit with abbreviated hash and message.

commands.desc: Visualize all branches as an ASCII graph
commands.tooltip: Shows all branches' commit history as an ASCII graph, making it easy to see where branches diverged and merged.

commands.desc: Show commits with their full diff patches
commands.tooltip: Shows each commit along with the full diff (all the code changes) it introduced.

commands.desc: Show the last 10 commits
commands.tooltip: Shows only the most recent 10 commits.

commands.desc: Filter log to commits by a specific author
commands.tooltip: Filters the commit history to show only commits made by a specific author.

commands.desc: Show commits from the past 2 weeks
commands.tooltip: Shows only commits made within the last 2 weeks.

commands.desc: Filter commits whose messages contain a keyword
commands.tooltip: Searches commit messages for a keyword and shows only matching commits.

commands.desc: Show commits that modified a specific file
commands.tooltip: Shows only commits that touched a specific file.

commands.desc: Show unstaged changes in the working directory
commands.tooltip: Shows changes you've made that haven't been staged yet — what's different from the last commit.

commands.desc: Show staged changes ready to be committed
commands.tooltip: Shows the changes you've staged (added with git add) that will go into the next commit.

commands.desc: Show diff between two branches
commands.tooltip: Shows the difference between two branches — what changes exist in one but not the other.

commands.desc: Show diff between last two commits
commands.tooltip: Shows what changed between the second-to-last commit and the last commit. HEAD~1 means 'one commit before HEAD'.

commands.desc: Show details and diff of a specific commit
commands.tooltip: Shows the details of a specific commit: its message, author, date, and all the file changes it introduced.

commands.desc: Show the content of a file at HEAD
commands.tooltip: Shows what a specific file looked like at HEAD (the latest commit), without needing to check out that commit.

commands.desc: Create a lightweight tag at HEAD
commands.tooltip: Creates a lightweight tag (a named pointer) to the current commit. Tags are often used to mark releases.

commands.desc: Create an annotated tag with a message
commands.tooltip: Creates an annotated tag with a message — stored as a full Git object. Better for releases than lightweight tags.

commands.desc: Delete a local tag
commands.tooltip: Deletes a local tag. The tag on the remote must be deleted separately.

commands.desc: List all remotes with their fetch and push URLs
commands.tooltip: Lists all configured remote repositories and their URLs for fetching and pushing.

commands.desc: Add a new remote named origin
commands.tooltip: Adds a new remote connection named 'origin' pointing to a GitHub repository URL.

commands.desc: Change the URL of an existing remote
commands.tooltip: Updates the URL of an existing remote — useful when a repository is moved.

commands.desc: Remove a remote from the repository
commands.tooltip: Removes a remote from the repository's configuration.

commands.desc: Set the commit author name for the current repo
commands.tooltip: Sets your name for commits in this repository. Git records the author name with every commit.

commands.desc: Set the commit author email
commands.tooltip: Sets your email address for commits in this repository.

commands.desc: Set global default editor for git messages
commands.tooltip: Sets the text editor that Git opens when you need to write a commit message or do an interactive rebase.

commands.desc: Show who last modified each line of a file
commands.tooltip: Shows who last modified each line of a file and in which commit — useful for tracking down when a change was introduced.

commands.desc: Show blame for lines 10 through 20 only
commands.tooltip: Shows git blame output for only lines 10 through 20.

commands.desc: Start a binary search through commits to find a bug
commands.tooltip: Starts a binary search through commit history to find which commit introduced a bug.

commands.desc: Mark a commit as good during bisect
commands.tooltip: Marks a specific commit as 'working correctly' during bisect. Git uses this to narrow the search.

commands.desc: Mark a commit as bad during bisect
commands.tooltip: Marks HEAD (the current commit) as 'broken' during bisect.

commands.desc: End bisect session and return to original HEAD
commands.tooltip: Ends the bisect session and returns HEAD to its original position.

commands.desc: Apply changes from a specific commit to current branch
commands.tooltip: Applies the changes from a single specific commit to the current branch, without merging the whole branch.

commands.desc: Apply a range of commits to current branch
commands.tooltip: Applies a range of commits (from abc1234 up to and including def5678) to the current branch.

commands.desc: Undo last commit keeping changes staged
commands.tooltip: Undoes the last commit but keeps the changes staged — as if you staged but hadn't committed yet. --soft is the gentlest undo.

commands.desc: Undo last commit keeping changes unstaged
commands.tooltip: Undoes the last commit and unstages the changes — files are modified but not staged. --mixed is the default.

commands.desc: Undo last commit and discard all changes
commands.tooltip: Undoes the last commit AND discards all changes permanently. --hard is destructive — the changes are gone.

commands.desc: Create a new commit that undoes a previous commit
commands.tooltip: Creates a new commit that reverses the changes of a specific past commit — safe because it doesn't rewrite history.

commands.desc: Revert the most recent commit
commands.tooltip: Reverts the most recent commit by creating an undo commit on top.

commands.desc: Remove untracked files and directories
commands.tooltip: Permanently deletes all untracked files and directories that aren't in .gitignore. -f=force, -d=directories.

commands.desc: Dry-run git clean to preview what would be removed
commands.tooltip: Shows what git clean would delete without actually deleting anything. -n means 'dry run'.

commands.desc: Export the latest commit as a zip file
commands.tooltip: Packages the current HEAD as a zip file. Useful for distributing a release without the .git metadata.

commands.desc: Show a log of all HEAD movements including resets and rebases
commands.tooltip: Shows a log of every place HEAD has pointed — including resets and rebases. Invaluable for recovering 'lost' commits.

commands.desc: Show the reflog for a specific branch
commands.tooltip: Shows the reflog for a specific branch instead of HEAD.

commands.desc: Add a submodule to the repository
commands.tooltip: Adds a submodule — a separate Git repository embedded inside the current one. Used for including dependencies.

commands.desc: Initialize submodule configuration in .git/config
commands.tooltip: Initializes submodule settings in .git/config after cloning a repo that contains submodules.

commands.desc: Fetch and checkout all submodules recursively
commands.tooltip: Downloads and checks out all submodules (and their nested submodules) after cloning.

commands.desc: Update all submodules to latest remote commits
commands.tooltip: Updates all submodules to the latest commit on their tracked remote branch.

commands.desc: Run a git command in every submodule
commands.tooltip: Runs 'git pull' inside every submodule, updating them all at once.

commands.desc: Check out a branch into a separate working directory
commands.tooltip: Creates a separate working directory for a different branch — lets you work on two branches simultaneously without stashing.

commands.desc: List all worktrees for this repository
commands.tooltip: Lists all linked worktrees associated with this repository.

commands.desc: Remove a linked worktree
commands.tooltip: Removes a worktree that's no longer needed.

commands.desc: Rewrite history removing a file from all commits
commands.tooltip: Rewrites the entire repository history removing a file from every commit — use for removing accidentally committed secrets.

commands.desc: Attach a note to a specific commit
commands.tooltip: Attaches a note (metadata) to a commit. Notes are separate from the commit message and can be changed without rewriting history.

commands.desc: Display notes attached to a specific commit
commands.tooltip: Displays the notes attached to a specific commit.

commands.desc: Summarize commit counts per author, sorted by count
commands.tooltip: Shows a summary of how many commits each author has made, sorted by count.

commands.desc: Commit count per author excluding merge commits
commands.tooltip: Same as git shortlog -sn but excludes merge commits from the count.

commands.desc: Describe current commit relative to nearest tag
commands.tooltip: Describes the current commit relative to the nearest tag: e.g. 'v1.0.0-5-gabcdef' means 5 commits after tag v1.0.0.

commands.desc: Describe commit appending -dirty if working tree modified
commands.tooltip: Like git describe but uses the commit hash if no tags exist, and appends '-dirty' if there are uncommitted changes.

commands.desc: Run garbage collection to optimize repository storage
commands.tooltip: Cleans up the repository's internal storage: compresses objects, removes loose objects, and optimizes the database.

commands.desc: Run aggressive garbage collection for maximum optimization
commands.tooltip: Runs a more thorough garbage collection — takes longer but produces better compression.

commands.desc: Verify the integrity of the Git object database
commands.tooltip: Checks the internal integrity of the Git object database to make sure nothing is corrupted.

commands.desc: Find dangling commits and blobs not reachable from any ref
commands.tooltip: Finds objects that exist in the Git database but aren't reachable from any branch or tag — potentially recoverable commits.

commands.desc: List files tracked by the Git index
commands.tooltip: Lists all files that Git is tracking (knows about) in the current index.

commands.desc: List untracked files that would be shown in git status
commands.tooltip: Lists files that are NOT tracked by Git (untracked) and not excluded by .gitignore.

commands.desc: Tell git to ignore changes to a tracked file locally
commands.tooltip: Tells Git to stop reporting changes to a tracked file — the file stays in the repo but local changes are ignored.

commands.desc: Stop ignoring local changes to a tracked file
commands.tooltip: Re-enables change tracking for a file you previously marked as assume-unchanged.

commands.desc: Interactively rebase last 5 commits to squash/reorder
commands.tooltip: Like git rebase -i HEAD~3 but for 5 commits — opens an interactive editor to rewrite, reorder, or squash commits.

commands.desc: Create a fixup commit targeting a specific commit
commands.tooltip: Creates a special 'fixup!' commit that's marked to be squashed into the target commit during an interactive rebase.

commands.desc: Rebase and automatically squash fixup commits
commands.tooltip: Runs an interactive rebase and automatically moves fixup! commits to squash with their targets. --autosquash handles the arrangement.

commands.desc: Search all commit trees for sensitive strings in history
commands.tooltip: Searches through all commits across all branches for a specific string — useful for finding where a secret was accidentally committed.

commands.desc: Automate bisect using a test script to find regression
commands.tooltip: Automates the bisect process: runs pytest tests on each commit and marks it good/bad based on the test result.

commands.desc: Authenticate the gh CLI with GitHub interactively
commands.tooltip: Authenticates the gh command-line tool with your GitHub account via a browser-based flow.

commands.desc: Remove stored GitHub credentials
commands.tooltip: Removes stored GitHub credentials and logs you out of the gh tool.

commands.desc: Show the current GitHub authentication status
commands.tooltip: Shows which GitHub account gh is authenticated as and what permissions it has.

commands.desc: Create a new public GitHub repository
commands.tooltip: Creates a new public GitHub repository directly from the command line.

commands.desc: Create a new private GitHub repository
commands.tooltip: Creates a new private GitHub repository (only you and collaborators can see it).

commands.desc: Clone a GitHub repository to the local machine
commands.tooltip: Clones a GitHub repository to your local machine using the gh tool.

commands.desc: Fork a GitHub repository to your account
commands.tooltip: Creates a personal copy (fork) of someone else's GitHub repository under your account.

commands.desc: View repository details in the terminal
commands.tooltip: Displays repository information (description, stars, forks, etc.) in the terminal.

commands.desc: Open the current repository in a web browser
commands.tooltip: Opens the current repository's GitHub page in your web browser.

commands.desc: List all repositories in your GitHub account
commands.tooltip: Lists all repositories in your GitHub account.

commands.desc: Create a new GitHub issue
commands.tooltip: Creates a new issue in the current repository with the given title and description.

commands.desc: List open issues in the current repository
commands.tooltip: Lists all open issues in the current repository.

commands.desc: List closed issues
commands.tooltip: Lists closed (resolved) issues.

commands.desc: View details of issue #42
commands.tooltip: Shows the full details of issue number 42 in the terminal.

commands.cmd: gh issue comment 42 -b 'This is fixed in #45'
commands.desc: Add a comment to an issue
commands.tooltip: Posts a comment on an issue. -b means 'body' — the comment text.

commands.desc: Close issue #42
commands.tooltip: Closes issue #42, marking it as resolved.

commands.desc: Create a new pull request
commands.tooltip: Creates a new pull request (a proposal to merge your code changes into the main branch).

commands.desc: List open pull requests in the repository
commands.tooltip: Lists all currently open pull requests.

commands.desc: View details of pull request #15
commands.tooltip: Shows the full details of pull request number 15.

commands.desc: Merge pull request #15 using squash merge
commands.tooltip: Merges a pull request using the 'squash' strategy — combines all commits into one before merging.

commands.desc: Approve pull request #15
commands.tooltip: Submits an approval review for a pull request, signaling it's ready to merge.

commands.desc: Check out a pull request branch locally
commands.tooltip: Downloads and checks out the branch of a pull request locally so you can test it.

commands.desc: Show status of PRs related to the current branch
commands.tooltip: Shows the status of pull requests related to the branch you're currently on.

commands.desc: List GitHub Actions workflows in the repository
commands.tooltip: Lists all GitHub Actions workflow files in the repository.

commands.desc: Trigger a workflow run manually
commands.tooltip: Manually triggers a workflow that supports the workflow_dispatch event.

commands.desc: List recent workflow runs
commands.tooltip: Lists recent GitHub Actions workflow runs and their statuses.

commands.desc: View details of a specific workflow run
commands.tooltip: Shows logs and details for a specific workflow run identified by its ID.

commands.desc: Create a public GitHub Gist from a file
commands.tooltip: Creates a GitHub Gist (a shareable snippet) from a local file. Public means anyone can see it.

commands.desc: List your GitHub Gists
commands.tooltip: Lists all your GitHub Gists.

commands.desc: Create a new GitHub release
commands.tooltip: Creates a new GitHub release with a tag, title, and release notes. Releases are versioned snapshots.

commands.desc: List all releases in the repository
commands.tooltip: Lists all published releases in the repository.

commands.desc: Set a repository secret for GitHub Actions
commands.tooltip: Sets an encrypted repository secret used by GitHub Actions workflows.

commands.desc: List secret names in the repository
commands.tooltip: Lists the names of all secrets set in the repository (values are never shown).

commands.desc: Search GitHub repositories with filters
commands.tooltip: Searches GitHub for repositories matching a query, filtered by programming language.

commands.desc: Run an nginx container (pulls image if not present)
commands.tooltip: Downloads the nginx image if needed and starts it as a container. A container is an isolated, portable environment for running software.

commands.desc: Run container in detached (background) mode
commands.tooltip: Starts the container in detached mode — it runs in the background and you keep your terminal. -d means 'detached'.

commands.desc: Run container interactively with a bash shell
commands.tooltip: Starts a container you can interact with — you get a command prompt inside it. -i=interactive, -t=terminal.

commands.desc: Run container mapping host port 8080 to container port 80
commands.tooltip: Maps port 8080 on your host machine to port 80 inside the container, so you can access the service at localhost:8080.

commands.desc: Mount a host directory into the container
commands.tooltip: Mounts a folder from your host machine into the container. Changes in the folder are visible inside the container.

commands.desc: Run container with a specific name
commands.tooltip: Names the container 'mycontainer' so you can refer to it by name instead of a random ID.

commands.desc: Run container and auto-remove it when it exits
commands.tooltip: Runs a container that automatically deletes itself when it stops — no cleanup needed.

commands.desc: Run container with an environment variable set
commands.tooltip: Sets an environment variable inside the container. Programs in the container can read MY_VAR to get 'value'.

commands.desc: Run container using host networking
commands.tooltip: Bypasses Docker's network isolation — the container shares the host's network stack directly.

commands.desc: Limit container memory to 512 megabytes
commands.tooltip: Prevents the container from using more than 512 megabytes of RAM.

commands.desc: Build an image from Dockerfile in current directory
commands.tooltip: Builds a Docker image from the Dockerfile in the current directory (.). -t assigns a name and version tag.

commands.desc: Build using a specific Dockerfile
commands.tooltip: Builds using a specific Dockerfile instead of the default 'Dockerfile'. -f specifies the filename.

commands.desc: Build image without using layer cache
commands.tooltip: Forces a fresh build by ignoring any cached layers — every instruction runs from scratch.

commands.desc: Pass a build argument to the Dockerfile
commands.tooltip: Passes a build-time variable (like a version number) into the Dockerfile's ARG instructions.

commands.desc: Download a Docker image from Docker Hub
commands.tooltip: Downloads a Docker image (ubuntu version 22.04) from Docker Hub to your local machine.

commands.desc: Push a local image to Docker Hub
commands.tooltip: Uploads a local image to Docker Hub so others can download it.

commands.desc: List all locally stored Docker images
commands.tooltip: Lists all Docker images stored on your machine.

commands.desc: List all images including intermediate layers
commands.tooltip: Lists all images including intermediate build layers (not just final tagged images).

commands.desc: Remove a local Docker image
commands.tooltip: Deletes a local image. You'll need to remove containers using it first.

commands.desc: Remove all dangling (untagged) images
commands.tooltip: Removes all dangling images — images that aren't tagged and aren't used by any container. Good for freeing space.

commands.desc: List currently running containers
commands.tooltip: Lists only the containers that are currently running.

commands.desc: List all containers including stopped ones
commands.tooltip: Lists all containers, including stopped ones.

commands.desc: List only container IDs of running containers
commands.tooltip: Lists only the container IDs (not full info) of running containers — useful for scripting.

commands.desc: Gracefully stop a running container (sends SIGTERM)
commands.tooltip: Sends SIGTERM to a container, giving it time to shut down cleanly (like asking it to stop).

commands.desc: Start a stopped container
