commands.desc: Start Gemini CLI with an inline MCP server for filesystem access
commands.tooltip: Starts Gemini CLI with an inline MCP filesystem server, giving Gemini read/write access to the filesystem via the protocol.

commands.desc: Create a custom /review slash command by adding a .toml file in the Gemini commands directory
commands.tooltip: Creates a custom /review slash command by adding a .toml configuration file in the Gemini commands directory.

commands.desc: Define a custom Gemini CLI slash command in TOML format with a name, description, and prompt template
commands.tooltip: Shows the TOML format for defining a custom Gemini CLI slash command with a name, description, and prompt template.

commands.desc: Set your Gemini API key so Gemini CLI can authenticate without browser login
commands.tooltip: Sets your Gemini API key as an environment variable so Gemini CLI can authenticate without a browser login.

commands.desc: Alternative env var for the Gemini API key (also accepted by Gemini CLI)
commands.tooltip: An alternative environment variable name for the Gemini API key — both GEMINI_API_KEY and GOOGLE_API_KEY are accepted.

commands.desc: Set the GCP project ID for Vertex AI-backed Gemini usage
commands.tooltip: Sets the Google Cloud project ID for using Gemini through Vertex AI (enterprise Google Cloud backend).

commands.desc: Route Gemini CLI requests through Vertex AI instead of Google AI Studio
commands.tooltip: Routes all Gemini CLI requests through Vertex AI (Google Cloud) instead of the default Google AI Studio endpoint.

commands.desc: Set the default model for all Gemini CLI sessions via environment variable
commands.tooltip: Sets the default Gemini model for all sessions via an environment variable — overrides the built-in default.

commands.desc: Enable sandbox mode by default for all Gemini CLI sessions
commands.tooltip: Enables sandbox mode for all Gemini CLI sessions by default via an environment variable.

commands.desc: View the full Gemini CLI configuration file (model, theme, MCP servers, etc.)
commands.tooltip: Views the full Gemini CLI settings.json file showing model, theme, MCP server config, and other preferences.

commands.desc: Configure default theme and model in settings.json — edits persist across all sessions
commands.tooltip: Shows the JSON snippet for setting the default theme and model in settings.json — these persist across sessions.

commands.desc: Enable Docker-based sandbox mode in settings.json for isolated code execution
commands.tooltip: Shows the JSON snippet for enabling Docker-based sandbox mode in settings.json for isolated code execution.

commands.desc: Enable token caching in settings.json to reduce latency and cost on repeated context
commands.tooltip: Shows the JSON config for enabling token caching — reuses cached context (like large codebases) to cut cost and latency.

commands.desc: Start Gemini CLI in sandbox mode — all shell commands run inside a container
commands.tooltip: Starts Gemini CLI with --sandbox so all shell tool calls run inside an isolated container instead of your real system.

commands.desc: Pre-pull the Docker image used by Gemini CLI sandbox mode
commands.tooltip: Pre-pulls the Docker image used by Gemini CLI sandbox mode so the first sandboxed run starts instantly.

commands.cmd: # Sandbox uses Docker by default; falls back to Podman if Docker unavailable
commands.desc: Gemini CLI sandbox automatically detects Docker or Podman for code execution isolation
commands.tooltip: Explains that Gemini CLI sandbox auto-detects Docker or falls back to Podman for container-based code isolation.

commands.cmd: # Enable tokenCache in settings.json for repeated large-context sessions
commands.desc: Token caching reuses cached prompt context (e.g. large codebases) to save cost and latency
commands.tooltip: Explains the token caching feature — repeated large-context sessions reuse cached prompts to save cost and speed up responses.

commands.desc: Enable checkpointing to save and restore long-running agentic sessions
commands.tooltip: Starts Gemini CLI with --checkpointing so long multi-step agentic sessions can be saved and resumed later.

commands.desc: Gemini CLI saves conversation checkpoints so you can resume complex multi-step tasks
commands.tooltip: Explains that Gemini CLI saves session checkpoints to ~/.config/gemini/checkpoints/ so you can restore complex tasks.

commands.cmd: gemini -p 'Add input validation to all API routes in @src/routes/'
commands.desc: Ask Gemini to edit multiple source files in a directory with a single prompt
commands.tooltip: Asks Gemini to edit multiple source files across a directory with a single prompt — Gemini uses its Edit/WriteFile tools automatically.

commands.cmd: gemini -p 'Write unit tests for @src/auth.py and save them to tests/test_auth.py'
commands.desc: Generate a new test file based on an existing source file using Gemini CLI
commands.tooltip: Asks Gemini to generate a new test file based on an existing source file and save it to a specific path.

commands.cmd: gemini -p 'Explain the architecture of this project' @src/ @README.md
commands.desc: Use multiple @ context references to ask Gemini a high-level project question
commands.tooltip: Uses multiple @ context references (source directory + README) to ask Gemini a high-level architecture question.

commands.cmd: gemini -p 'Find all TODO comments and create a task list'
commands.desc: Use Gemini's SearchText tool to scan the codebase and summarize todos
commands.tooltip: Asks Gemini to scan the codebase using its SearchText tool to find all TODO comments and summarize them.

commands.cmd: gemini -p 'Review this PR diff and suggest improvements' < diff.patch
commands.desc: Pipe a git diff into Gemini CLI for code review feedback
commands.tooltip: Pipes a git diff file into Gemini CLI to get AI-powered code review feedback on the changes.

commands.cmd: git diff HEAD~1 | gemini -p 'Summarize what changed in this commit'
commands.desc: Pipe the latest git diff to Gemini CLI for an automatic commit summary
commands.tooltip: Pipes the diff of the latest commit to Gemini and asks it to write a human-readable summary of the changes.

commands.cmd: gemini -p 'Scaffold a new FastAPI endpoint for /users/profile' @src/
commands.desc: Generate new code that follows the patterns of your existing codebase
commands.tooltip: Asks Gemini to scaffold a new endpoint that follows the patterns already in your existing source directory.

commands.desc: Inline shell command substitution to pass error logs as dynamic prompt content
commands.tooltip: Uses shell command substitution $(...) inside the prompt to dynamically inline error log content, then passes the source file as context so Gemini can diagnose the error.

commands.desc: Initialize a scoped package under an org namespace (e.g. @myorg/package)
commands.tooltip: Creates a package.json whose name starts with @myorg/ — scoped packages act like namespaces on the npm registry, preventing name collisions with other packages.

commands.desc: Set a default author name used in every new npm init
commands.tooltip: Saves your name in npm's global config so every future 'npm init' pre-fills the author field — a one-time convenience setup.

commands.desc: Set the default license written into new package.json files
commands.tooltip: Saves a default license to npm's global config so new projects automatically declare MIT (or whatever you set) without you having to type it every time.

commands.desc: Show all active npm configuration settings and their sources
commands.tooltip: Prints every npm setting currently in effect, showing which file each value came from (.npmrc, environment variable, or built-in default).

commands.desc: Print npm configuration as a JSON object for scripting
commands.tooltip: Same as 'npm config list' but formats the output as a JSON object — easier to parse in scripts or pipe into other tools.

commands.desc: Print the directory where global npm packages are installed
commands.tooltip: Shows the folder where globally installed npm packages land — useful for diagnosing PATH issues when a global command isn't found.

commands.desc: Set the npm package registry URL — useful when switching to a private registry
commands.tooltip: Tells npm where to download packages from. Normally points to the public npmjs.com registry; change this when using a company's private registry like Artifactory or Nexus.

commands.desc: Point npm at the GitHub Package Registry instead of the public npmjs.com registry
commands.tooltip: Redirects npm to GitHub's Package Registry — used in organizations that host private packages on GitHub instead of npmjs.com.

commands.desc: Remove a specific key from your npm configuration
commands.tooltip: Removes a key from your .npmrc configuration file. Useful for cleaning up old proxy or registry settings.

commands.desc: Open the global .npmrc config file in your default text editor
commands.tooltip: Opens your global .npmrc file in the default editor. .npmrc is a plain-text INI file that controls npm's behavior for all projects on the machine.

commands.desc: Make npm pin every installed package to an exact version instead of a range like ^1.2.3
commands.tooltip: Makes npm record exact versions like '2.3.1' instead of ranges like '^2.3.1' in package.json. Exact pins give fully reproducible installs without a lockfile.

commands.desc: Suppress the funding messages shown after installs
commands.tooltip: Hides the 'X packages are looking for funding' message that appears after installs. Purely cosmetic — disabling it doesn't affect anything.

commands.desc: Reduce npm install output to warnings and errors only
commands.tooltip: Reduces install output to warnings and errors only, cutting out the verbose progress spam. Silent makes sense in CI logs.

commands.desc: Set a custom directory where npm stores its download cache
commands.tooltip: Sets the folder where npm stores downloaded package tarballs. Pointing this to a faster or larger drive can speed up repeated installs.

commands.desc: Install a package and add it to dependencies in package.json
commands.tooltip: Downloads a package from the npm registry and adds it to the 'dependencies' section of package.json — these are packages needed at runtime.

commands.desc: Install a package as a devDependency — only used during development, not shipped to production
commands.tooltip: Installs a package and adds it to 'devDependencies' — tools only needed during development (testing frameworks, linters, bundlers) that shouldn't be shipped to production.

commands.desc: Install a package globally so its command is available anywhere in your terminal
commands.tooltip: Installs a package globally on your machine so its CLI command is available in any directory. You won't find it in node_modules — it goes to a system-wide folder.

commands.desc: Install a package pinned to an exact version with no wiggle room (no ^ or ~ range)
commands.tooltip: Installs a package and writes its exact version number (no ^ or ~ prefix) to package.json. Useful when you want reproducible installs without relying on a lockfile.

commands.desc: Install ignoring peer dependency conflicts — useful when older packages haven't updated their peer ranges
commands.tooltip: Installs while ignoring peer dependency conflicts. Peer deps are 'you must also have X installed' requirements; --legacy-peer-deps skips enforcing them — helpful when upgrading React or other frameworks where the ecosystem is still catching up.

commands.desc: Force reinstall even if the package already exists, bypassing cache checks
commands.tooltip: Forces npm to re-download and reinstall even if the package is already present locally, ignoring the cache. Useful for resolving corrupted installs.

commands.desc: Install a package into node_modules without adding it to package.json
commands.tooltip: Downloads the package into node_modules but doesn't write anything to package.json. Good for quick experiments you don't want to commit.

commands.desc: Use the local download cache when possible instead of hitting the network
commands.tooltip: Tries to use your local npm cache before making network requests. Speeds up installs and works without internet when the package was previously downloaded.

commands.desc: Install only the dependencies needed to run the app, skipping devDependencies
commands.tooltip: Installs only the 'dependencies' listed in package.json, skipping 'devDependencies'. Keeps your Docker production image or server smaller.

commands.desc: Install an exact specific version of a package
commands.tooltip: Installs exactly version 2.3.1 of a package — useful when a specific version is known to work and newer versions have breaking changes.

commands.desc: Install the newest published version of a package
commands.tooltip: Installs the most recently published version, even if it's a major upgrade. Useful for getting the absolute newest release.

commands.desc: Install the version tagged 'beta' on the npm registry
commands.tooltip: Installs whatever version the publisher tagged as 'beta' — a pre-release that may be unstable but has features you want to try.

commands.desc: Install a package directly from a GitHub repository
commands.tooltip: Installs a package directly from a GitHub repository. Useful for using a fork or a package that isn't published to the npm registry.

commands.desc: Install from a specific branch of a GitHub repository
commands.tooltip: Installs from a specific branch of a GitHub repo — handy when you need unreleased code from a feature branch.

commands.desc: Install a package from a local directory on your machine
commands.tooltip: Installs a package from a folder on your own machine. Common when developing a library alongside the app that uses it.

commands.desc: Install a sibling folder as a local dependency using a relative path
commands.tooltip: Same as the local path install but uses the file: protocol prefix — both forms work identically.

commands.desc: Install a package from a direct tarball URL
commands.tooltip: Installs a package from a direct tarball URL — the same .tgz files npm normally downloads behind the scenes.

commands.desc: Clean install from package-lock.json exactly — faster and stricter than npm install, ideal for CI/CD pipelines
commands.tooltip: Performs a clean install strictly from package-lock.json without updating it. Faster than 'npm install' and fails loudly if the lockfile is out of sync — the gold standard for CI/CD pipelines.

commands.desc: Clean install production dependencies only, skipping devDependencies — use in Docker production builds
commands.tooltip: Like 'npm ci' but skips devDependencies. Use this in your Dockerfile's production stage to get a lean image with only runtime code.

commands.desc: Install a package without running its postinstall scripts — useful for security
commands.tooltip: Prevents npm from running the package's install scripts (postinstall, preinstall). Reduces supply-chain attack risk from malicious packages running arbitrary code on install.

commands.desc: Update a single package to the latest allowed version
commands.tooltip: Upgrades one specific package to the latest version within its range in package.json.

commands.desc: Remove a package from node_modules and from package.json
commands.tooltip: Removes a package from node_modules and deletes its entry from package.json — keeps things tidy.

commands.desc: Remove a globally installed package
commands.tooltip: Removes a globally installed CLI package from your machine.

commands.desc: Remove a package and clean it from devDependencies
commands.tooltip: Removes a package and specifically cleans it from the devDependencies section.

commands.desc: Flatten the node_modules tree to remove duplicate copies of the same package
commands.tooltip: Restructures node_modules to eliminate duplicate copies of the same package that ended up at different nesting levels — reduces disk usage.

commands.desc: Remove packages from node_modules that are not listed in package.json
commands.tooltip: Deletes packages from node_modules that are installed but not listed in package.json — cleans up leftovers from manual deletions or old installs.

commands.desc: Remove devDependencies from node_modules — useful before building a Docker image
commands.tooltip: Removes devDependencies from node_modules. Run this before packaging a Docker image to strip test tools and reduce image size.

commands.desc: List all available scripts defined in package.json
commands.tooltip: Lists every script name defined in the 'scripts' object in package.json so you know what's available to run.

commands.desc: Run the 'lint' script to check code for style and syntax issues
commands.tooltip: Runs the linter (usually ESLint) to check your code for style violations, unused variables, and potential bugs — without actually running the code.

commands.desc: Run the 'format' script to auto-format code with a tool like Prettier
commands.tooltip: Runs the formatter (usually Prettier) to automatically rewrite your code files to match the configured style rules.

commands.desc: Run the build script and pass extra flags after -- directly to the underlying tool
commands.tooltip: Runs the build script and passes '--watch' to the underlying tool (e.g. webpack) so it rebuilds automatically whenever you change a file. Everything after -- is forwarded.

commands.desc: Shortcut for 'npm run start' — typically launches the app in production mode
commands.tooltip: Shortcut for 'npm run start' — conventionally starts the app in production mode, often running 'node server.js'.

commands.desc: Run tests and pass --watch to the test runner (e.g., Jest) for continuous re-runs
commands.tooltip: Runs tests and forwards '--watch' to the test runner (like Jest) which then re-runs tests automatically as you edit files.

commands.desc: Run the 'stop' script from package.json
commands.tooltip: Runs the 'stop' script — typically shuts down a server process started by 'npm start'.

commands.desc: Run the stop script then the start script
commands.tooltip: Runs the stop script and then the start script in sequence — a quick way to bounce the app.

commands.desc: Manually trigger the preinstall lifecycle hook script
commands.tooltip: Manually triggers the 'preinstall' lifecycle hook. npm runs this automatically before installing dependencies — useful for checks or setup steps.

commands.desc: Manually trigger the postbuild lifecycle hook — npm runs this automatically after 'npm run build'
commands.tooltip: Manually triggers the 'postbuild' hook. npm calls this automatically after 'npm run build' completes — useful for copying files or running post-processing.

commands.desc: Bump the patch version (1.0.0 → 1.0.1), commit the change, and create a git tag automatically
commands.tooltip: Bumps the third number in your version (1.2.3 → 1.2.4), saves it to package.json, commits the change, and creates a git tag — all in one command. Patch = bug fixes.

commands.desc: Bump the minor version (1.0.0 → 1.1.0), commit, and tag — use for backward-compatible new features
commands.tooltip: Bumps the middle version number (1.2.3 → 1.3.0) and creates a git tag. Minor = new features that don't break existing users.

commands.desc: Bump the major version (1.0.0 → 2.0.0), commit, and tag — use for breaking changes
commands.tooltip: Bumps the first number (1.2.3 → 2.0.0) and creates a git tag. Major = changes that break backwards compatibility.

commands.desc: Bump to a prerelease version like 1.0.0-beta.1
commands.tooltip: Creates a prerelease version like '1.2.3-beta.1' — used for releasing test versions before a stable release.

commands.desc: Bump the version in package.json without creating a git commit or tag
commands.tooltip: Bumps the version number in package.json but does NOT commit to git or create a tag — useful when you manage commits yourself.

commands.desc: Publish a scoped package (like @org/pkg) publicly — scoped packages are private by default
commands.tooltip: Scoped packages (like @myorg/package) are private by default on npm. This flag makes them publicly installable by anyone.

commands.desc: Simulate a publish and show exactly what files would be uploaded, without actually sending anything
commands.tooltip: Simulates publishing and shows you a list of files that would be uploaded — lets you verify your .npmignore is correct before actually publishing.

commands.desc: Publish a version and tag it as 'beta' instead of 'latest'
commands.tooltip: Publishes the package but labels it 'beta' instead of 'latest'. Users won't get it via 'npm install package' unless they explicitly ask for '@beta'.

commands.desc: Create a .tgz tarball of the package exactly as it would appear on npm — useful for testing before publishing
commands.tooltip: Bundles your package into a .tgz tarball exactly as it would appear on npm. Install this tarball locally to test your package before publishing.

commands.desc: Mark a published package version as deprecated with a warning message shown to installers
commands.tooltip: Marks an old published version as deprecated so anyone trying to install it sees your warning message — keeps the ecosystem clean without removing code.

commands.desc: Remove a specific published version from npm (restricted to within 72 hours of publishing)
commands.tooltip: Deletes a published version from the npm registry. Only allowed within 72 hours of publishing — after that, npm prevents deletion to protect other projects depending on it.

commands.desc: Assign a dist-tag (like 'latest' or 'beta') to a specific published version
commands.tooltip: Tags a specific published version with a label like 'latest' or 'beta'. The 'latest' tag is what 'npm install package' downloads by default.

commands.desc: List all dist-tags and their versions for a package
commands.tooltip: Lists all named tags for a package — shows you which versions are labeled 'latest', 'beta', 'next', etc.

commands.desc: Remove a dist-tag from a package
commands.tooltip: Removes a dist-tag label from a package without deleting the version itself.

commands.desc: Log in or create an npm account — stores credentials in .npmrc
commands.tooltip: Creates or logs into an npm account, saving your auth token to .npmrc so npm can publish packages on your behalf.

commands.desc: Authenticate with the npm registry (same as npm adduser)
commands.tooltip: Authenticates with the npm registry. Same as 'npm adduser' — logs you in and saves a token locally.

commands.desc: Remove your npm authentication token from .npmrc
commands.tooltip: Removes your npm authentication token from .npmrc, effectively logging you out.

commands.desc: Print the username you are currently logged in as on npm
commands.tooltip: Prints your current npm username — a quick way to confirm you're logged in as the right account before publishing.

commands.desc: List all authentication tokens associated with your npm account
commands.tooltip: Lists all active authentication tokens for your npm account so you can audit what has access.

commands.desc: Create a new npm access token for use in CI/CD pipelines
commands.tooltip: Generates a new npm token for use in CI/CD pipelines or automated scripts that need to publish packages without a browser login.

commands.desc: Revoke a specific npm authentication token
commands.tooltip: Permanently invalidates an npm auth token — do this immediately if a token is accidentally exposed.

commands.desc: Add another npm user as a co-owner/maintainer of a package
commands.tooltip: Grants another npm user publish rights to your package — useful for team-maintained packages.

commands.desc: Remove a maintainer from an npm package
commands.tooltip: Removes a user's publish access to a package.

commands.desc: List all maintainers of an npm package
commands.tooltip: Lists all users who have permission to publish the package.

commands.desc: Make a scoped package publicly visible on npm
commands.tooltip: Makes a scoped package publicly visible and installable on npm.

commands.desc: Make a scoped package private, restricting access to authorized users
commands.tooltip: Restricts a scoped package to only authenticated, authorized users — effectively makes it private.

commands.desc: Show full registry metadata for a package including versions, license, dependencies, and maintainers
commands.tooltip: Fetches and prints the full metadata record for a package from the npm registry: description, versions, license, maintainers, dependencies, and more.

commands.desc: Print just the latest published version number of a package
commands.tooltip: Quickly prints just the latest version number — handy in scripts or when you want to check without visiting a browser.

commands.desc: List every published version of a package as a JSON array
commands.tooltip: Returns a JSON array of every version ever published for a package — useful for seeing the full history.

commands.desc: Get the direct download URL for a package's tarball from the registry
commands.tooltip: Prints the direct download URL for the package tarball — useful for scripted downloads or airgapped environments.

commands.desc: Search the npm registry for packages matching a keyword
commands.tooltip: Searches the npm registry and returns a list of matching packages with name, description, author, and download counts.

commands.desc: Show the runtime dependencies declared by a published package
commands.tooltip: Shows what packages this package itself requires at runtime — the first level of its dependency tree.

commands.desc: Show what peer dependencies a package requires you to install alongside it
commands.tooltip: Shows what peer deps a package requires — packages you must install alongside it but that it won't install automatically (e.g. React for a component library).

commands.desc: Open a package's source code repository URL in your browser
commands.tooltip: Opens the package's source code repository (usually GitHub) in your default browser — useful for reading source or filing issues.

commands.desc: Open a package's homepage or documentation in your browser
commands.tooltip: Opens the package's official homepage or docs site in your browser.

commands.desc: Open a package's bug tracker (GitHub Issues, etc.) in your browser
commands.tooltip: Opens the package's issue tracker in your browser — the fastest way to report a bug.

commands.desc: Open documentation for a package in your browser
commands.tooltip: Opens the documentation URL for a package in your browser.

commands.desc: Show the full nested dependency tree including deeply nested transitive dependencies
commands.tooltip: Prints the complete dependency tree including packages required by your packages — can be very long for large projects.

commands.desc: Show where a specific package appears within the full dependency tree
commands.tooltip: Shows every location in the dependency tree where a specific package appears — helpful for understanding why duplicates exist.

commands.desc: List all globally installed packages at the top level
commands.tooltip: Lists all packages you've installed globally, one level deep — a quick inventory of your global CLI tools.

commands.desc: Explain why a package is installed and which dependency chain required it
commands.tooltip: Explains which package in your tree depends on a given package and through what chain — answers 'why is this in my node_modules?'

commands.desc: Show outdated packages with extended details including package homepage
commands.tooltip: Same as 'npm outdated' but adds a homepage column so you can quickly visit each package's site for release notes.

commands.desc: Fix all audit vulnerabilities including breaking-change major version upgrades — use with caution
commands.tooltip: Like 'npm audit fix' but will also apply major version upgrades to fix vulnerabilities even if they might break your app. Review changes carefully.

commands.desc: Output the security audit results as JSON for parsing in CI or custom tooling
commands.tooltip: Outputs the full audit report as machine-readable JSON — useful for piping into security tools or custom dashboards.

commands.desc: Only fail the audit check for vulnerabilities of high or critical severity
commands.tooltip: Makes 'npm audit' only exit with an error code for high/critical vulnerabilities, ignoring low/moderate ones. Common in CI to avoid blocking on minor issues.

commands.desc: Show packages in your dependency tree that are seeking funding from their authors
commands.tooltip: Shows which of your installed packages are actively seeking financial support through Open Collective, GitHub Sponsors, or other platforms.

commands.desc: Show only file names that differ between two versions of a package
commands.tooltip: Shows just the filenames that differ between two versions of a package — a quick overview before reading the full diff.

commands.desc: Show a diff of source code changes between two published versions of a package
commands.tooltip: Shows a unified diff (like git diff) of source file changes between two specific published versions of a package.

commands.desc: Use CSS-like selector syntax to query packages in your dependency tree by semver range
commands.tooltip: Uses CSS-inspired selector syntax to query and filter packages in your dependency tree — for example, find all packages above a certain version.

commands.desc: Link a previously 'npm link'd global package into the current project — replaces the installed version
commands.tooltip: Connects the globally symlinked local package into node_modules of the current project, replacing the registry version. Changes to the local package appear immediately.

commands.desc: Remove a globally symlinked package from the current project
commands.tooltip: Removes the symlink and restores the registry-published version. Run this when you're done testing your local changes.

commands.desc: Verify the integrity of npm's cache and garbage-collect stale entries
commands.tooltip: Checks all cached packages for corruption and removes garbage entries. Less drastic than a full clean — run this first if installs are failing.

commands.desc: Pre-populate the cache with a package without installing it into node_modules
commands.tooltip: Pre-downloads a package into the cache without installing it. Useful for seeding the cache before going offline.

commands.desc: Create a new workspace package inside a monorepo at the specified path
commands.tooltip: Creates a new sub-package at the given path and registers it as a workspace in the root package.json — the start of a monorepo sub-package.

commands.desc: Install dependencies for all workspaces in a monorepo from the root
commands.tooltip: Installs dependencies for every workspace package in the monorepo from the root — all node_modules are hoisted and deduplicated.

commands.desc: Run a script in one specific workspace package without affecting others
commands.tooltip: Runs the build script only for one specific workspace package while leaving others untouched.

commands.desc: Run the test script across every workspace that defines it, skipping those that don't
commands.tooltip: Runs the test script across every workspace, but gracefully skips any that don't define a test script.

commands.desc: Execute a command inside the environment of a specific workspace package
commands.tooltip: Executes a command inside a specific workspace's environment — the command resolves binaries from that workspace's node_modules.

commands.desc: Install a dependency into a specific workspace package rather than the root
commands.tooltip: Adds a dependency to one specific workspace package rather than the monorepo root. The package appears in that workspace's package.json.

commands.desc: Run a locally installed binary (jest) with npm exec — preferred over npx for scripts
commands.tooltip: Runs a locally installed binary (jest) using npm's exec. Preferred over npx in package.json scripts because it always uses the local version.

commands.desc: Read a specific field from package.json without opening the file
commands.tooltip: Reads the 'name' field from package.json and prints it — useful in shell scripts that need to know the package name without parsing JSON manually.

commands.desc: Print all scripts defined in package.json
commands.tooltip: Prints the entire 'scripts' object from package.json — a quick way to see all available commands.

commands.desc: Programmatically set a field in package.json from the command line
commands.tooltip: Writes a new value to a field in package.json from the command line — scriptable and avoids the need for text editing or jq.

commands.desc: Remove a field from package.json using the command line
commands.tooltip: Removes a specific field from package.json. Handy for cleaning up generated files or automating package.json edits in scripts.

commands.desc: Generate a Software Bill of Materials listing all installed dependencies and their licenses
commands.tooltip: Generates a Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) — a complete inventory of all dependencies and their licenses. Required by some enterprise security standards and government contracts.

commands.desc: Bootstrap a new Next.js project using the latest version of the scaffolding tool
commands.tooltip: Scaffolds a Next.js project using the latest version of the tool. Next.js is a React framework with server-side rendering and routing built in.

commands.desc: Create a new Vite project — Vite is a fast modern bundler for React, Vue, Svelte, etc.
commands.tooltip: Scaffolds a new project using Vite — a modern build tool much faster than webpack, supporting React, Vue, Svelte, and vanilla JS.

commands.desc: Run a package from npm without installing it, auto-confirming the install prompt with --yes
commands.tooltip: Runs a package without installing it and auto-accepts the download prompt. The --yes flag skips the 'is it okay to download this?' confirmation.

commands.desc: Run a specific package binary in a temporary environment using -p to specify the package
commands.tooltip: Uses -p to specify which npm package to pull in, then runs its binary. Here it runs TypeScript's compiler init without a global TypeScript install.

commands.desc: Instantly serve files in the current directory over HTTP without a global install
commands.tooltip: Instantly launches a simple static web server in the current folder. Great for previewing HTML files or testing a frontend build without a real server setup.

commands.desc: Serve a build output folder (e.g. 'dist') as a static website for quick local preview
commands.tooltip: Serves a compiled app folder (like 'dist' from a Vite/webpack build) as a static website — useful for testing production builds locally.

commands.desc: Execute a TypeScript file directly with ts-node without compiling to JavaScript first
commands.tooltip: Runs a TypeScript file directly in Node.js without a build step. ts-node compiles TypeScript on the fly — great for quick scripts.

commands.desc: Auto-format all files in the project with Prettier without a global install required
commands.tooltip: Reformats every file in the project to match Prettier's style rules. A quick way to standardize code style without a global install.

commands.desc: Run ESLint with automatic fixing on a directory without needing a global ESLint install
commands.tooltip: Runs ESLint with automatic fixing — corrects fixable style issues across an entire folder without needing ESLint installed globally.

commands.desc: Type-check a TypeScript project and report errors without writing any output files
commands.tooltip: Type-checks a TypeScript project and reports any type errors but doesn't write any .js output files. Fast way to validate types in CI.

commands.desc: Delete node_modules and dist cross-platform — works on Windows too, unlike bare rm -rf
commands.tooltip: Deletes folders without worrying about OS differences. On Windows 'rm -rf' doesn't work, but rimraf does — a cross-platform delete utility.

commands.desc: Set environment variables cross-platform before running a command — needed on Windows
commands.tooltip: Sets environment variables before running a command in a way that works on Windows, Mac, and Linux — Windows uses different syntax (set instead of export).

commands.desc: Run tests with Jest and generate a code coverage report without a global install
commands.tooltip: Runs your Jest test suite and generates a code coverage report showing which lines of your source code are hit by tests.

commands.cmd: npx concurrently 'npm run server' 'npm run client'
commands.desc: Run multiple npm scripts simultaneously in the same terminal window
commands.tooltip: Runs multiple commands at the same time in one terminal, with color-coded output for each. Ideal for starting a backend and frontend server together.

commands.desc: Wait for a URL or port to become available — useful in CI to wait for a server to start
commands.tooltip: Blocks until a URL or port is responding — useful in CI scripts to wait for a dev server to start before running tests against it.

commands.desc: Open a URL in the default browser cross-platform from the command line
commands.tooltip: Opens a URL in your default browser from the terminal in a cross-platform way (works on Mac, Linux, and Windows).

commands.desc: Update all version strings in package.json to the latest available versions and write the changes
commands.tooltip: Scans your package.json and rewrites version strings to the absolute latest available versions — then run 'npm install' to actually apply the upgrades.

commands.desc: Analyze your project and report unused dependencies and missing packages
commands.tooltip: Analyzes your code and package.json to find packages you installed but never import, and packages you import but forgot to list — keeps dependencies tidy.

commands.desc: Apply local patches to node_modules packages and persist them across installs
commands.tooltip: Applies patches you've made to files inside node_modules and re-applies them after every 'npm install' — a sanctioned way to fix bugs in third-party packages.

commands.desc: Detect circular dependencies in your JavaScript or TypeScript source files
commands.tooltip: Detects circular import chains in your JS/TS code. Circular dependencies can cause subtle bugs and make code harder to reason about.

commands.desc: List the open-source licenses of all installed dependencies — useful for compliance
commands.tooltip: Lists the open-source licenses of every package in node_modules — important for legal compliance in commercial software.

commands.desc: Check that your compiled JS/CSS bundles don't exceed configured size limits
commands.tooltip: Checks that your compiled JavaScript/CSS files don't exceed configured size limits — prevents accidental performance regressions from bloated bundles.

commands.desc: Visualize what code is inside your compiled JavaScript bundle by size
commands.tooltip: Reads the source map for your bundle and shows an interactive treemap of every module and its contribution to total bundle size.

commands.desc: Run a JavaScript file with Node.js
commands.tooltip: Executes a JavaScript file using the Node.js runtime on your machine.

commands.desc: Execute a one-liner JavaScript expression directly from the terminal without a file
commands.tooltip: Runs a JavaScript one-liner directly in the terminal without needing a file. -e means 'evaluate this string as code'.

commands.desc: Print the result of a JS expression — -p is like -e but auto-prints the return value
commands.tooltip: Like -e but also prints the return value. Useful for quickly extracting data in shell scripts.

commands.desc: Start a Node.js script with the Chrome DevTools debugger attached for step-through debugging
commands.tooltip: Starts the script with Chrome DevTools debugging enabled. Open chrome://inspect in Chrome to connect and use breakpoints, watch variables, and step through code.

commands.desc: Start with debugger attached and pause at the very first line before any user code runs
commands.tooltip: Same as --inspect but pauses at the very first line of code. Gives you time to attach DevTools before any code runs.

commands.desc: Load environment variables from a .env file natively without dotenv package (Node 20+)
commands.tooltip: Loads key=value pairs from a .env file into process.env without needing the 'dotenv' package. Built into Node.js 20+ natively.

commands.desc: Restart the script automatically whenever the file changes — built-in alternative to nodemon (Node 18+)
commands.tooltip: Restarts the script automatically when the file is saved. Node 18+'s built-in alternative to the popular 'nodemon' package.

commands.desc: Watch an entire directory for changes and restart the script when any file changes (Node 19+)
commands.tooltip: Watches an entire directory for changes and restarts the entry script when any file inside changes (Node 19+).

commands.desc: Increase Node's V8 heap to 4 GB — useful for memory-intensive build tools or large datasets
commands.tooltip: Raises Node's V8 JavaScript heap memory limit from the default ~1.5 GB to 4 GB. Needed for memory-hungry build tools or data processing scripts.

commands.desc: Enable experimental VM modules — required to use Jest with native ES modules (ESM)
commands.tooltip: Enables experimental VM module support — required when using Jest with native ES module (import/export) syntax.

commands.desc: Load TypeScript files with ts-node as an ESM loader without a separate compile step
commands.tooltip: Uses ts-node as a module loader to run TypeScript files as ES modules — a modern alternative to ts-node's CJS mode.

commands.desc: Pre-require the dotenv package to load .env variables before your script runs
commands.tooltip: Pre-requires (loads before the main script) the dotenv package so .env variables are available from the very first line of your code.

commands.desc: Tell Node to treat piped stdin code as ES module syntax rather than CommonJS
commands.tooltip: Tells Node to treat code piped via stdin as ES module syntax instead of CommonJS — necessary if your piped code uses 'import'.

commands.desc: Print the installed Node.js version number
commands.tooltip: Prints your installed Node.js version — the quick way to check before reporting bugs or verifying a deployment.

commands.desc: Print the full Node.js version string
commands.tooltip: Same as 'node -v' — prints the full version string like 'v20.11.0'.

commands.desc: Read JavaScript from stdin and execute it — useful for piping scripts
commands.tooltip: Reads JavaScript from stdin and executes it — useful for piping code: 'echo "console.log(42)" | node -'

commands.desc: Exit Node immediately with a specific exit code — useful in shell scripts to signal failure
commands.tooltip: Exits Node immediately with exit code 1 (error). Shell scripts use exit codes to detect failures — 0 means success, anything else means failure.

commands.desc: Quick one-liner to check a system value using a built-in Node module
commands.tooltip: A quick way to evaluate a Node expression that uses a built-in module — prints the number of CPU cores here.

commands.desc: Enable the experimental built-in fetch API in older Node versions before it was stable
commands.tooltip: Enables the browser-compatible fetch() API in older Node versions before it was made stable in Node 18.

commands.desc: Install the latest Long-Term Support (LTS) version of Node.js — most stable for production
commands.tooltip: Downloads and installs the latest Long-Term Support (LTS) Node.js release. LTS versions get security patches for 30 months — the safe choice for production.

commands.desc: Install an exact specific Node.js version
commands.tooltip: Downloads a specific patch version of Node.js — useful when you need to match an exact runtime environment.

commands.desc: Switch to the latest installed LTS version of Node.js
commands.tooltip: Switches to the latest LTS version you have installed without needing to type the exact version number.

commands.desc: Switch to whichever version you set as your nvm default
commands.tooltip: Switches to whichever version you've aliased as 'default' — the one that opens in new terminal sessions.

commands.desc: List all Node.js versions available to download from the internet
commands.tooltip: Fetches and lists every Node.js version available for download from nodejs.org — can be a long list.

commands.desc: List all available LTS (Long-Term Support) Node.js releases
commands.tooltip: Shows only the LTS (Long-Term Support) release lines available for download — the named releases like 'Iron', 'Hydrogen', 'Gallium'.

commands.desc: Set a Node.js version as your default — it will be used in every new terminal session
commands.tooltip: Makes Node.js v20 your default — it will be active automatically whenever you open a new terminal.

commands.desc: Set the latest LTS as your default Node.js version for new sessions
commands.tooltip: Sets the latest installed LTS version as your default. The lts/* wildcard always resolves to the most recent LTS.

commands.desc: Print which Node.js version is currently active in this terminal
commands.tooltip: Prints which Node.js version is active in this terminal right now.

commands.desc: Uninstall a specific Node.js version that is no longer needed
commands.tooltip: Removes a specific Node.js version from nvm's storage to free up disk space.

commands.desc: Run a command using a specific Node.js version without permanently switching
commands.tooltip: Runs a command using a specific Node.js version for just that one command, without switching your session's active version.

commands.desc: Run a script with a specific Node.js version without changing the current session
commands.tooltip: Directly runs a script with a specific Node.js version — a shorter form of 'nvm exec 18 node script.js'.

commands.desc: Print the full file path to the node binary for a specific installed version
commands.tooltip: Prints the filesystem path to the node binary for version 20 — useful for pointing tools or Docker COPYs to the right binary.

commands.desc: Delete nvm's download cache to free disk space
commands.tooltip: Deletes nvm's tarball download cache. Safe to run any time — nvm will re-download versions as needed.

commands.desc: Restore the system's default PATH, removing nvm-managed node from the session
commands.tooltip: Removes nvm-managed node/npm from the current session's PATH, reverting to the system-installed Node.js if one exists.

commands.desc: Read the .nvmrc file that specifies which Node.js version this project expects
commands.tooltip: Reads the .nvmrc file — a one-line file containing a Node.js version number that tells nvm (and tools like CI) which version this project needs.

commands.desc: Create a .nvmrc file that locks this project to a specific Node.js major version
commands.tooltip: Creates or overwrites .nvmrc with a version string, locking the project to Node 20 for anyone using nvm.

commands.desc: In a folder with a .nvmrc file, switch to the exact version that file specifies
commands.tooltip: When run in a folder with a .nvmrc file, automatically switches to the version specified in that file — no need to type the version number.

commands.desc: Install the latest LTS Node.js version and set it as the default using Volta
commands.tooltip: Installs the latest LTS Node.js and sets it as your global default. Volta manages the installation automatically in its own toolchain directory.

commands.desc: Install a specific major version of Node.js with Volta
commands.tooltip: Installs Node.js v20 via Volta and sets it as the global default for your machine.

commands.desc: Install a specific npm version and pin it as the default with Volta
commands.tooltip: Installs npm version 10 globally via Volta. Volta ensures the right npm version is used wherever you are.

commands.desc: Install the latest Yarn version and make it globally available via Volta
commands.tooltip: Installs the latest stable Yarn and makes it available globally via Volta.

commands.desc: Pin a specific Node.js version to this project in package.json so every teammate uses the same version
commands.tooltip: Writes 'node@20' into the 'volta' field of package.json, locking this project to Node 20 for everyone who uses Volta — no .nvmrc or manual switching needed.

commands.desc: Pin a specific npm version to the current project so CI and teammates use the same npm
commands.tooltip: Pins a specific npm version to this project in package.json so all team members and CI use exactly the same npm.

commands.desc: List all tools and versions currently managed by Volta
commands.tooltip: Shows all tools and their versions currently managed by Volta on this machine.

commands.desc: List every version of every tool Volta has installed
commands.tooltip: Shows every version of every tool Volta has ever installed — useful for auditing disk usage.

commands.desc: Run a command with a specific Node.js version without changing the project or global default
commands.tooltip: Runs a one-off command with a specific Node.js version without changing the project pin or global default.

commands.desc: Print the file path Volta uses to resolve the 'node' command in this context
commands.tooltip: Shows the full path Volta resolves 'node' to in the current context — the project's pinned version or the global default.

commands.desc: Remove a specific Node.js version from Volta's toolchain store
commands.tooltip: Removes a specific Node.js version from Volta's local tool store.

commands.desc: Set up Volta's shims in your shell profile — run this after first installing Volta
commands.tooltip: Configures your shell (bash, zsh, fish) to use Volta's shims. Run once after installing Volta.

commands.desc: Download a Node.js version into Volta's cache without setting it as default
commands.tooltip: Downloads a Node.js version into Volta's cache in advance without activating it — useful for offline environments.

commands.desc: Install all dependencies from yarn.lock — equivalent to 'npm install' but using Yarn
commands.tooltip: Installs all dependencies listed in package.json using yarn.lock for exact versions — Yarn's equivalent of 'npm install'.

commands.desc: Install without updating yarn.lock — fails if lockfile would change, ideal for CI
commands.tooltip: Installs from yarn.lock without modifying it. Fails if the lockfile is out of sync with package.json — the correct way to install in CI.

commands.desc: Add a runtime dependency and update yarn.lock
commands.tooltip: Adds a package to your project's dependencies and updates yarn.lock.

commands.desc: Add a development dependency to the project
commands.tooltip: Adds a package to devDependencies — tools only needed during development.

commands.desc: Add a package as a peer dependency
commands.tooltip: Adds a package to peerDependencies — declaring that the host app must also install this package.

commands.desc: Add a package with a specific semver range to package.json
commands.tooltip: Adds a package with a specific semver range. The ^ means 'compatible with 2.x.x' — any minor or patch update is allowed.

commands.desc: Install a package globally on your machine (Yarn v1 classic)
commands.tooltip: Installs a package globally (Yarn v1 classic) so its CLI is available in every directory.

commands.desc: Remove a dependency from the project and update yarn.lock
commands.tooltip: Removes a package from the project and updates yarn.lock.

commands.desc: Upgrade a package to the latest version allowed by its semver range
commands.tooltip: Upgrades a package to the latest version allowed by its semver range in package.json.

commands.desc: Upgrade a package to the absolute latest version even if it exceeds the semver range
commands.tooltip: Upgrades a package to the absolute latest version on the registry, potentially crossing major version boundaries.

commands.desc: Open an interactive UI to choose which packages to upgrade — much easier than manual updates
commands.tooltip: Opens a terminal UI that lets you select which packages to upgrade using arrow keys and spacebar — much friendlier than running upgrade commands manually.

commands.desc: Interactively choose packages to upgrade to their absolute latest versions
commands.tooltip: Same interactive upgrade tool but considers the absolute latest versions, including major bumps.

commands.desc: Run a script defined in package.json
commands.tooltip: Runs a script defined in package.json's scripts object.

commands.desc: Run the 'dev' script from package.json (Yarn allows omitting 'run' for scripts)
commands.tooltip: Runs the 'dev' script. Yarn allows omitting 'run' for non-reserved script names.

commands.desc: Run the build script across all workspace packages in a Yarn monorepo
commands.tooltip: Runs 'build' in every workspace package of a Yarn monorepo — parallelizable in Berry with the '--parallel' flag.

commands.desc: Install dependencies for a specific workspace and its transitive deps only (Yarn Berry)
commands.tooltip: Installs only the dependencies needed for one workspace and its transitive deps — useful in Docker to avoid installing the whole monorepo.

commands.desc: Run a package from the npm registry temporarily without installing it — Yarn's equivalent of npx
commands.tooltip: Downloads and runs a package once without installing it — Yarn Berry's equivalent of npx.

commands.desc: Show why a package is installed — which other package in the tree depends on it
commands.tooltip: Traces back through the dependency tree to explain why a package ended up in your project — which dependency required it.

commands.desc: Show metadata about a package from the npm registry
commands.tooltip: Fetches and displays metadata about a package from the npm registry.

commands.desc: List installed packages filtered by a pattern — useful for finding all React-related packages
commands.tooltip: Lists installed packages that match a glob pattern — useful for finding all packages in the React ecosystem at once.

commands.desc: Create a global symlink for the current package for local development across projects
commands.tooltip: Creates a global symlink for the current package so other local projects can use it during development.

commands.desc: Link a locally symlinked package into the current project
commands.tooltip: Links a globally symlinked local package into the current project, overriding the registry version.

commands.desc: Remove a local link and revert to the registry version
commands.tooltip: Removes a local link and reverts to using the registry-published version.

commands.desc: Clear Yarn's local download cache to free disk space or fix corrupted cache issues
commands.tooltip: Deletes Yarn's local download cache. Run when packages are corrupt or installs are failing with integrity errors.

commands.desc: List all packages currently in Yarn's local cache
commands.tooltip: Lists every package currently stored in Yarn's local cache.

commands.desc: Verify that installed node_modules match the checksums in yarn.lock
commands.tooltip: Verifies that the packages in node_modules match the checksums recorded in yarn.lock — detects tampering or corruption.

commands.desc: Create a new package.json interactively using Yarn
commands.tooltip: Interactively creates a new package.json file for a project.

commands.desc: Initialize a Yarn Berry (v3+) project with modern zero-install features
commands.tooltip: Creates a Yarn Berry (v3+) project with modern features like Plug'n'Play (PnP) module resolution and zero-install support.

commands.desc: Upgrade the current project's Yarn version to the latest stable Berry release
commands.tooltip: Upgrades the Yarn version used in the current project to the latest stable Berry release and updates .yarnrc.yml.

commands.desc: Check which module resolution strategy this Yarn project uses (node-modules vs pnp)
commands.tooltip: Checks whether this project uses classic node_modules or Plug'n'Play (PnP) module resolution — the two modes behave differently.

commands.desc: Install all dependencies using pnpm's content-addressable store — fast and disk-efficient
commands.tooltip: Installs all dependencies using pnpm's content-addressable store — files are hard-linked rather than copied, making installs much faster and sharing disk space across projects.

commands.desc: Install from the lockfile exactly without updating it — use in CI/CD for reproducible builds
commands.tooltip: Installs from pnpm-lock.yaml exactly without any modifications — the right mode for CI where reproducibility is required.

commands.desc: Add a runtime dependency using pnpm
commands.tooltip: Adds a package to dependencies and updates pnpm-lock.yaml.

commands.desc: Add a development dependency using pnpm
commands.tooltip: Adds a package to devDependencies using pnpm.

commands.desc: Install a package globally with pnpm
commands.tooltip: Installs a package globally so its CLI is available in every directory.

commands.desc: Add a package at a specific version using pnpm
commands.tooltip: Adds a specific version of a package with pnpm.

commands.desc: Remove a dependency and update pnpm-lock.yaml
commands.tooltip: Removes a package from node_modules and package.json, updating the lockfile.

commands.desc: Update all packages to their latest allowed versions according to semver ranges
commands.tooltip: Updates all packages to the latest versions within their declared semver ranges.

commands.desc: Update all packages to their absolute latest versions, ignoring semver ranges
commands.tooltip: Updates all packages to the absolute latest versions regardless of the declared ranges — use with care as it may introduce breaking changes.

commands.desc: Interactively choose which packages to update
commands.tooltip: Opens an interactive UI to choose which packages to update — similar to Yarn's upgrade-interactive.

commands.desc: Run the build script from package.json using pnpm
commands.tooltip: Runs the build script from package.json using pnpm.

commands.desc: Run the dev script (pnpm allows omitting 'run' for most scripts)
commands.tooltip: Runs the dev script. Like Yarn, pnpm allows omitting 'run' for most script names.

commands.desc: Run the test script from package.json using pnpm
commands.tooltip: Runs the test script from package.json.

commands.desc: Run a scaffolding tool once without installing it globally — pnpm's equivalent of npx
commands.tooltip: Runs a scaffolding tool once without a global install — pnpm's equivalent of npx, uses the global pnpm store for caching.

commands.desc: Run a locally installed binary without using npx or adding it to scripts
commands.tooltip: Runs a locally installed binary using pnpm without needing npx or adding it to scripts.

commands.desc: Run the test script recursively across all packages in a pnpm monorepo workspace
commands.tooltip: Runs 'test' recursively across all workspace packages in a pnpm monorepo.

commands.desc: Run a script only in a specific package within a monorepo using the --filter flag
commands.tooltip: Runs a script in one specific workspace package using pnpm's --filter flag to target by name.

commands.desc: Remove packages from pnpm's global content store that are no longer referenced by any project
commands.tooltip: Removes packages from pnpm's global content store that are no longer used by any project on the machine — frees significant disk space in large monorepos.

commands.desc: Check the integrity of pnpm's global store and detect any modified or corrupt packages
commands.tooltip: Verifies the integrity of every package in pnpm's store — detects corruption or manual edits.

commands.desc: Print the path to pnpm's global content-addressable store on disk
commands.tooltip: Prints the path to pnpm's global store — usually ~/.pnpm-store or a platform-specific location.

commands.desc: Display the dependency tree of the current project
commands.tooltip: Shows the dependency tree of the current project.

commands.desc: Show which installed packages have newer versions available
commands.tooltip: Lists packages with newer versions available on the registry.

commands.desc: Check installed packages for known security vulnerabilities
commands.tooltip: Checks installed packages for known security vulnerabilities.

commands.desc: Attempt to automatically resolve security vulnerabilities found by pnpm audit
commands.tooltip: Attempts to automatically update packages to versions that resolve known security vulnerabilities.

commands.desc: Show why a package is installed — which dependency requires it
commands.tooltip: Explains why a package is installed by tracing which dependencies in your tree require it.

commands.desc: Link the current package into pnpm's global store for use in other projects
commands.tooltip: Registers the current package in pnpm's global store so other local projects can link to it for development.

commands.desc: Create a new package.json in the current directory using pnpm
commands.tooltip: Creates a new package.json in the current directory using pnpm.

commands.desc: Install and use the latest LTS Node.js via pnpm's built-in Node version manager
commands.tooltip: Downloads and sets the latest LTS Node.js version as the global default using pnpm's built-in node version manager.

commands.desc: Install and switch to a specific Node.js version using pnpm's node manager
commands.tooltip: Downloads and activates Node.js version 20 globally via pnpm's node manager — no nvm needed.

commands.desc: Configure pnpm to automatically install peer dependencies when adding packages
commands.tooltip: Configures pnpm to automatically install peer dependencies when you add a package, eliminating most peer dep warning noise.

commands.desc: Minimize the number of package versions in the lockfile by resolving version conflicts
commands.tooltip: Optimizes the lockfile to minimize the number of duplicate package versions, reducing node_modules size.

commands.desc: Install the Nginx web server on Ubuntu/Debian — the most popular open-source server for self-hosted apps
commands.tooltip: Nginx (pronounced 'engine-X') is a high-performance web server that can also act as a reverse proxy — sitting in front of your app and forwarding traffic to it. It's the most common way to expose a self-hosted app to the internet.

commands.desc: Start the Nginx service immediately
commands.tooltip: Starts Nginx immediately. After installing Nginx you need to explicitly start it — it doesn't run automatically until you also 'enable' it.

commands.desc: Configure Nginx to start automatically on every boot
commands.tooltip: Marks Nginx to auto-start when the server boots. Without this, Nginx stops working after every reboot and you'd have to SSH in to restart it manually.

commands.desc: Apply configuration changes without dropping active connections — safer than restart for production
commands.tooltip: Sends a signal to the running Nginx process to re-read its config files without interrupting active connections. Prefer this over restart in production.

commands.desc: Fully restart Nginx — use after major config changes or if reload doesn't take effect
commands.tooltip: Fully stops and restarts Nginx. Use this if reload doesn't pick up a change, like adding a new SSL certificate module.

commands.desc: Test all Nginx config files for syntax errors before applying — always run this before reloading
commands.tooltip: Dry-run test that parses every Nginx config file and reports any syntax errors. Always run this before reloading — a bad config can take your whole server offline.

commands.desc: Dump the full parsed Nginx configuration to stdout — useful for auditing what's actually active
commands.tooltip: Like 'nginx -t' but also dumps the fully merged configuration to stdout. Useful for verifying that include files and site configs are loaded as expected.

commands.desc: Print the installed Nginx version number
commands.tooltip: Prints the Nginx version. Knowing the version helps when looking up docs, changelogs, or checking whether a security patch applies to you.

commands.desc: Open the main Nginx configuration file for editing
commands.tooltip: The main Nginx config file sets global settings like worker processes, log paths, and included site configs. Most app-specific settings live in separate files under sites-available/.

commands.desc: Create or edit a site-specific virtual host (server block) configuration file
commands.tooltip: Each app gets its own config file here defining its domain, port, SSL certificate, and proxy settings. Files in sites-available/ are inactive until symlinked into sites-enabled/.

commands.desc: Enable a site by symlinking it into sites-enabled — Nginx only reads configs in that folder
commands.tooltip: Activates a site config by creating a symbolic link. Nginx only reads configs from sites-enabled/ — this pattern lets you enable/disable sites without deleting config files.

commands.desc: Disable a virtual host by removing its symlink without deleting the config file
commands.tooltip: Deactivates a site by removing its symlink from sites-enabled/. The config file in sites-available/ is preserved so you can re-enable it later.

commands.desc: Stream live Nginx access logs — shows every incoming request in real time
commands.tooltip: Streams every incoming request in real time — shows IP address, path, status code, and response size. Useful for watching traffic or debugging 404s.

commands.desc: Stream live Nginx error logs — first place to look when a site returns 502/504 or won't load
commands.tooltip: Shows Nginx error messages as they happen. When a page returns 502 Bad Gateway or 404, this log tells you exactly why.

commands.desc: View the default Nginx server block to understand the base configuration
commands.tooltip: Views the default catch-all server block. Understanding this helps you know what Nginx returns when no other site config matches a request.

commands.desc: Test the integrity of a rotated, compressed Nginx log file
commands.tooltip: Checks that a rotated, compressed log file isn't corrupt. Nginx log rotation is usually handled by logrotate automatically.

commands.desc: Install the Caddy web server — automatically provisions HTTPS certificates with zero config
commands.tooltip: Caddy is a web server that automatically obtains and renews free TLS certificates from Let's Encrypt — zero SSL config required. Great for simple self-hosted setups.

commands.desc: Print the installed Caddy version
commands.tooltip: Prints the Caddy version. Caddy updates frequently with security fixes, so it's worth checking you're on a recent build.

commands.desc: Start Caddy in the foreground using the Caddyfile in the current directory
commands.tooltip: Starts Caddy in the foreground — you'll see logs directly in the terminal. Use this for testing; use 'caddy start' or systemd for production.

commands.desc: Start Caddy as a background daemon
commands.tooltip: Starts Caddy as a background daemon. Use this when you don't want to tie Caddy to a terminal session.

commands.desc: Stop the running Caddy daemon
commands.tooltip: Gracefully shuts down the Caddy daemon.

commands.desc: Reload Caddy's configuration without restarting or dropping connections
commands.tooltip: Tells the running Caddy daemon to apply a new Caddyfile config. Connections stay alive during the reload.

commands.desc: Check the Caddyfile for syntax errors before applying it
commands.tooltip: Checks the Caddyfile for errors before applying it — equivalent to nginx -t. Prevents taking your server offline with a bad config.

commands.desc: Auto-format the Caddyfile to canonical style in-place
commands.tooltip: Reformats the Caddyfile to Caddy's canonical style in-place — like a code formatter but for your server config.

commands.desc: Edit the main Caddy configuration file — defines reverse proxies, static sites, and TLS settings
commands.tooltip: The Caddyfile is Caddy's human-friendly config language. A typical entry looks like: 'app.example.com { reverse_proxy localhost:3000 }' — that's all you need to proxy an app with HTTPS.

commands.desc: Follow Caddy's live log output via systemd's journal
commands.tooltip: Streams Caddy's systemd logs live. Caddy logs certificate renewal events, errors, and request info here.

commands.desc: Convert a Caddyfile to Caddy's native JSON config format for inspection
commands.tooltip: Converts a Caddyfile to Caddy's underlying JSON config format. Useful for understanding what Caddy is actually doing or for advanced API-based config management.

commands.desc: Run the Traefik reverse proxy in Docker — it auto-discovers other containers and routes traffic to them
commands.tooltip: Runs Traefik — a cloud-native reverse proxy that auto-discovers Docker containers via labels and routes traffic to them. Popular in Docker-heavy self-hosted setups.

commands.desc: Print the installed Traefik version
commands.tooltip: Prints the Traefik version.

commands.desc: Check whether the Traefik process is alive and responding
commands.tooltip: Checks whether the local Traefik process is alive. Used in Docker health checks and monitoring scripts.

commands.desc: Stream live Traefik logs — shows routing decisions, TLS cert renewals, and errors
commands.tooltip: Streams live Traefik logs — shows routing decisions, TLS certificate events, and errors. Your first stop when a service isn't reachable.

commands.desc: Query Traefik's dashboard API to see all active routers, services, and middleware
commands.tooltip: Queries Traefik's dashboard REST API for its full routing table — all routers, services, middlewares, and certificates in JSON format.

commands.desc: Ping Traefik's health endpoint — returns 200 OK when the proxy is healthy
commands.tooltip: Hits Traefik's health ping endpoint. Returns '200 OK' when Traefik is running and healthy — useful in uptime monitoring scripts.

commands.desc: Install Certbot and its Nginx plugin for automated free HTTPS certificate provisioning
commands.tooltip: Installs Certbot and its Nginx plugin. Certbot is the official Let's Encrypt client that automates free HTTPS certificate issuance and renewal.

commands.desc: Request a free Let's Encrypt TLS certificate and auto-configure Nginx to use it
commands.tooltip: The one-command HTTPS setup: requests a free certificate and automatically edits your Nginx config to use it. Certbot also sets up auto-renewal.

commands.desc: Get a certificate using Certbot's built-in web server (no Nginx/Apache needed) — stops any running web server first
commands.tooltip: Gets a certificate without touching any running web server — Certbot briefly spins up its own server on port 80. Useful for custom server setups.

commands.desc: Renew all certificates that expire within 30 days — safe to run anytime
commands.tooltip: Renews any certificate expiring within 30 days. This is typically run by a daily cron job or systemd timer that Certbot installs automatically.

commands.desc: Simulate a certificate renewal to verify the process works without actually changing anything
commands.tooltip: Simulates the renewal process end-to-end without actually updating any files. Run this to verify your renewal configuration is correct.

commands.desc: List all certificates managed by Certbot with their domains and expiry dates
commands.tooltip: Lists every certificate Certbot manages with its associated domains and expiry date. A quick inventory of your TLS setup.

commands.desc: Remove a certificate and all its files from the system
commands.tooltip: Removes a certificate and its associated files from the system — use when decommissioning a domain.

commands.desc: Revoke a certificate with the CA — use when a private key may have been compromised
commands.tooltip: Tells Let's Encrypt's CA to mark a certificate as revoked — browsers will immediately distrust it. Use when a private key is compromised.

commands.desc: Check the exact expiry date of a TLS certificate file directly with OpenSSL
commands.tooltip: Directly reads the certificate file and prints its expiry date. Useful in monitoring scripts that alert you before a cert expires.

commands.cmd: openssl s_client -connect example.com:443 -servername example.com 2>/dev/null | openssl x509 -noout -dates
commands.desc: Connect to a live HTTPS server and print the certificate's validity window
commands.tooltip: Connects to a live HTTPS server and prints its certificate's validity dates. Checks what the server is actually serving, not just what's on disk.

commands.desc: List all certificate sets managed by Let's Encrypt on this server
commands.tooltip: Lists all active certificate directories. Each domain gets its own folder containing cert.pem, privkey.pem, and chain.pem.

commands.desc: Show whether UFW (Uncomplicated Firewall) is active and list all current rules
commands.tooltip: Shows whether UFW is active and lists all firewall rules. Always check this before and after making changes.

commands.desc: Show UFW status with full rule details including logging level and default policies
commands.tooltip: Same as 'ufw status' but includes the default input/output/forward policies and logging level.

commands.desc: Enable UFW and start enforcing firewall rules — do this only after allowing SSH to avoid locking yourself out
commands.tooltip: Turns on the firewall and begins enforcing rules. Make sure port 22 (SSH) is allowed BEFORE running this on a remote server or you'll lock yourself out.

commands.desc: Disable UFW and stop enforcing all firewall rules — traffic flows freely
commands.tooltip: Turns off the firewall — all traffic is allowed. Use temporarily for debugging connectivity issues.

commands.desc: Open port 22 for SSH connections — always do this before enabling UFW on a remote server
commands.tooltip: Opens port 22 for SSH. This is the first rule you should add to UFW on any server you access remotely.

commands.desc: Allow inbound HTTP traffic on port 80
commands.tooltip: Opens port 80 for HTTP traffic — required for web servers and for Certbot's HTTP-01 challenge during certificate issuance.

commands.desc: Allow inbound HTTPS traffic on port 443
commands.tooltip: Opens port 443 for HTTPS — required for all TLS-secured web traffic.

commands.desc: Allow both HTTP and HTTPS through UFW using Nginx's named application profile
commands.tooltip: Uses Nginx's named UFW profile to open both ports 80 and 443 in one command. UFW reads available profiles from /etc/ufw/applications.d/.

commands.desc: Block MySQL's default port from external access — keep databases off the public internet
commands.tooltip: Blocks MySQL's port from the internet. Databases should never be publicly accessible — apps connect to them locally or over a private network.

commands.desc: Allow all traffic from your local network subnet — useful for internal-only admin interfaces
commands.tooltip: Allows all traffic from a private LAN subnet. Use this to grant access to admin panels only from your home network.

commands.cmd: sudo ufw allow from 1.2.3.4 to any port 22
commands.desc: Restrict SSH access to a single trusted IP address
commands.tooltip: Locks SSH access to one specific IP address — drastically reduces your attack surface from the entire internet to one machine.

commands.desc: Remove a specific UFW allow rule
commands.tooltip: Removes a rule by specifying it exactly as it was added. You can also use 'ufw delete [rule number from status numbered]'.

commands.desc: Wipe all UFW rules and reset to defaults — a clean slate if configuration gets messy
commands.tooltip: Removes every UFW rule and disables the firewall. A last resort when rules are so tangled that starting over is cleaner.

commands.desc: Enable UFW logging so blocked connections appear in /var/log/ufw.log
commands.tooltip: Enables firewall logging. Blocked connection attempts appear in /var/log/ufw.log — useful for auditing intrusion attempts.

commands.desc: List all application profiles available for UFW rules (Nginx, OpenSSH, Apache, etc.)
commands.tooltip: Lists named application profiles available for UFW rules. App profiles are defined in /etc/ufw/applications.d/ and bundle ports into named groups.

commands.desc: Rate-limit SSH connections to block brute-force login attempts — automatically bans IPs with too many failed attempts
commands.tooltip: Implements connection-rate limiting on SSH — automatically blocks IPs that attempt more than 6 connections in 30 seconds. A simple brute-force defence.

commands.desc: List all active iptables rules with packet/byte counters — the low-level firewall underlying UFW
commands.tooltip: Dumps all active iptables chains and rules with packet counts. UFW uses iptables under the hood — this shows the raw rules UFW generates.

commands.desc: Allow inbound TCP connections on port 80 at the iptables level
commands.tooltip: Directly adds an iptables rule to accept inbound TCP on port 80. Use UFW for day-to-day management; use iptables directly for advanced scenarios.

commands.desc: Save current iptables rules to a file so they persist across reboots
commands.tooltip: Writes the current iptables ruleset to a file. Without this, all rules are lost on reboot. The 'iptables-persistent' package restores them automatically.

commands.desc: Restore iptables rules from a previously saved file
commands.tooltip: Loads iptables rules from a saved file — used on boot or after a reset to restore your firewall configuration.

commands.desc: Create a new systemd unit file to run your app as a managed background service
commands.tooltip: Creates a systemd unit file — a simple text file that tells Linux how to start, stop, and restart your application as a managed service with automatic restarts and logging.

commands.desc: Tell systemd to reload unit files from disk — required after creating or editing a .service file
commands.tooltip: Tells systemd to re-scan /etc/systemd/system/ for new or changed unit files. You must run this after creating or editing any .service file before the changes take effect.

commands.desc: Enable and immediately start a systemd service in one command
commands.tooltip: Combines 'enable' (start on boot) and 'start' (start right now) into a single command — the most common way to activate a new service.

commands.desc: Show the running status, recent logs, and process info for a systemd service
commands.tooltip: Shows whether the service is running, its process ID, memory usage, and the last few log lines. The first command to run when diagnosing a broken service.

commands.desc: Stop a running systemd service
commands.tooltip: Stops a running service gracefully by sending it a SIGTERM signal then waiting for it to exit.

commands.desc: Prevent a systemd service from starting automatically on boot
commands.tooltip: Removes the symlink that causes the service to start at boot. The service keeps running until stopped but won't survive a reboot.

commands.desc: Show the last 100 log lines from a specific systemd service
commands.tooltip: Prints the last 100 log lines from a service without pausing. Pipe-friendly — great for scripts and remote log review.

commands.desc: Follow a service's logs in real time via systemd journal
commands.tooltip: Live-tails a service's log output. Equivalent to 'tail -f' but for systemd-managed services whose output goes to the journal.

commands.desc: View a service's logs from the past hour
