commands.tooltip: Shows logs from the past hour for a specific service. journalctl supports human-readable time ranges like '2 hours ago', 'yesterday', or specific timestamps.

commands.desc: List all currently running systemd services
commands.tooltip: Lists every currently running systemd service. Useful for auditing what's active on a fresh server.

commands.desc: List all systemd services that have failed — start debugging here
commands.tooltip: Lists services that tried to start but failed. Start here when debugging a server that isn't behaving as expected after a reboot.

commands.desc: Create a drop-in override file for a systemd service without modifying the original unit
commands.tooltip: Creates a drop-in config file at /etc/systemd/system/myapp.service.d/override.conf that adds or overrides settings without touching the original unit file. Safe to use with package-managed services.

commands.desc: Pull latest images and recreate containers — the one-command update workflow for self-hosted apps
commands.tooltip: The standard self-hosted app update command — pulls fresh images and recreates containers if the image has changed. Safe to run as a cron job.

commands.desc: Follow live logs from a single named service in the Compose stack
commands.tooltip: Streams logs from just one service in the stack. Use this when you know which container is misbehaving.

commands.desc: Restart a single service within a running Compose stack without touching the others
commands.tooltip: Restarts one service container without touching the others. Useful after changing environment variables or secrets.

commands.desc: Open a bash shell inside a running Compose service container
commands.tooltip: Opens an interactive bash shell inside a running container. You can inspect files, run database queries, or debug environment variables from inside.

commands.desc: Rebuild all service images from scratch, ignoring the layer cache — ensures fresh builds
commands.tooltip: Rebuilds custom images from Dockerfiles ignoring the layer cache. Use this when dependency changes aren't being picked up.

commands.desc: Run multiple instances of a service for load balancing or redundancy
commands.tooltip: Runs multiple replicas of a service. Requires a load balancer (like Traefik or Nginx) in front to distribute traffic between them.

commands.desc: Create a persistent Docker volume for Portainer to store its configuration and settings
commands.tooltip: Creates a named Docker volume for Portainer's database and settings. Named volumes persist even if the container is removed and recreated.

commands.desc: Run Portainer CE — a web GUI for managing Docker containers, images, volumes, and networks
commands.tooltip: Installs and runs Portainer — a web-based Docker management GUI. It accesses Docker via /var/run/docker.sock and provides a visual interface for containers, stacks, volumes, and networks.

commands.desc: Open a shell inside the Portainer container for debugging
commands.tooltip: Opens a shell inside the Portainer container for low-level debugging. Portainer uses Alpine Linux so /bin/sh is available but bash may not be.

commands.desc: Pull the latest Portainer Community Edition image to update it
commands.tooltip: Pulls the latest Portainer CE image. After pulling, recreate the container with the same run command to update it.

commands.desc: Send a HEAD request to check if an app is running and responding on a local port
commands.tooltip: Sends a HEAD request and prints only the response headers — a quick way to confirm a service is running and see its status code without downloading the body.

commands.desc: Verbose curl request showing TLS handshake details and response headers — useful for debugging HTTPS setups
commands.tooltip: Verbose TLS debug output showing the certificate chain, cipher suite, and HTTP response headers. Use this when HTTPS isn't working.

commands.desc: Test Nginx virtual host routing by spoofing the Host header from the command line
commands.tooltip: Tests Nginx virtual host routing by sending a request to localhost but with a fake Host header. Nginx routes based on Host — this simulates how it handles a real request.

commands.desc: List all TCP ports currently listening on the server with the process name — see what's actually bound
commands.tooltip: Shows all listening TCP sockets with the process that owns each one. Faster and more detailed than netstat. Use this to see what services are actually bound to which ports.

commands.desc: Check specifically if something is listening on port 80
commands.tooltip: Checks whether anything is listening on port 80. If Nginx fails to start because 'address already in use', this reveals what's holding the port.

commands.desc: Show all listening TCP ports and the processes behind them (older alternative to ss)
commands.tooltip: Lists listening network ports and associated processes. Older tool, not installed by default on modern Ubuntu — use 'ss' instead if available.

commands.desc: Show which process is using HTTPS port 443
commands.tooltip: Shows which process is using the HTTPS port. Run this when Nginx or Caddy fails to bind to 443.

commands.desc: Explicitly request the IPv4 address (A record) for a domain
commands.tooltip: Explicitly requests IPv4 A records. Useful when you want to distinguish between A (IPv4) and AAAA (IPv6) responses.

commands.desc: Query a domain's IPv6 (AAAA) record
commands.tooltip: Queries for IPv6 address records. Needed when your hosting provider offers IPv6 and you want to verify DNS is set up for it.

commands.desc: Retrieve TXT records — used for domain verification, SPF, DKIM, and DMARC email authentication
commands.tooltip: Fetches TXT records used for domain verification (Google, GitHub), SPF email policy, DKIM keys, and DMARC policy records.

commands.desc: Find which name servers are authoritative for a domain
commands.tooltip: Returns the authoritative nameservers for a domain. If DNS changes aren't propagating, verify you're checking the authoritative server here.

commands.desc: Trace the full DNS delegation path from root servers down to the authoritative nameserver — deep debugging
commands.tooltip: Traces the complete DNS resolution path from root servers through TLDs to the authoritative nameserver. The deepest DNS debugging tool.

commands.desc: View the local hosts file — entries here override DNS and map hostnames to IPs on this machine only
commands.tooltip: The hosts file maps hostnames to IP addresses locally and is checked before DNS. Entries here can override or supplement DNS for any hostname.

commands.desc: Edit the local hosts file to add custom hostname-to-IP mappings (e.g. for local dev or internal services)
commands.tooltip: Edit the local hosts file to add custom mappings — like pointing 'db.local' to a server IP for internal use without DNS setup.

commands.desc: View the DNS resolver configuration — shows which DNS servers this machine queries
commands.tooltip: Shows which DNS servers this machine is configured to use. On modern Ubuntu this is usually managed by systemd-resolved rather than edited directly.

commands.desc: Show detailed DNS resolver status per network interface — modern replacement for /etc/resolv.conf inspection
commands.tooltip: Shows DNS resolution status per network interface, which servers are being used, and whether DNSSEC is active — the modern way to inspect DNS config.

commands.desc: Install WireGuard — a fast, modern VPN built into the Linux kernel
commands.tooltip: Installs WireGuard VPN. WireGuard is built into the Linux kernel since version 5.6 and is significantly simpler and faster than OpenVPN or IPsec.

commands.cmd: wg genkey | tee privatekey | wg pubkey > publickey
commands.desc: Generate a WireGuard private/public key pair — do this for each peer in the VPN
commands.tooltip: Generates a WireGuard key pair. The private key stays on this device; you share the public key with other peers. Never share private keys.

commands.desc: Create or edit the WireGuard interface configuration file
commands.tooltip: The WireGuard interface config defines this device's private key, listen port, IP address, and the list of peers with their public keys and allowed IPs.

commands.desc: Bring up the WireGuard VPN tunnel interface named wg0
commands.tooltip: Brings up the WireGuard virtual network interface defined in wg0.conf. Traffic to addresses in the VPN subnet is now tunneled through WireGuard.

commands.desc: Take down the WireGuard tunnel interface — disconnects the VPN
commands.tooltip: Tears down the WireGuard interface and removes the associated routes. Disconnects from the VPN.

commands.desc: Enable the WireGuard tunnel to start automatically on boot
commands.tooltip: Creates a systemd service for the wg0 interface so WireGuard reconnects automatically after every reboot.

commands.desc: Show the status of all active WireGuard interfaces, peers, and their last handshake times
commands.tooltip: Shows all active WireGuard interfaces, their listening ports, public keys, connected peers, and the time of the last handshake — a quick VPN status check.

commands.desc: Print the full configuration of the wg0 interface including all peer settings
commands.tooltip: Prints the running configuration of the wg0 interface including all peers. Useful for auditing or backing up your VPN config.

commands.desc: Authenticate cloudflared with your Cloudflare account to create tunnels
commands.tooltip: Opens a browser to authenticate with Cloudflare and stores a certificate locally that authorizes you to create tunnels.

commands.desc: Create a named Cloudflare Tunnel — generates credentials that let traffic reach your server without opening firewall ports
commands.tooltip: Registers a named tunnel with Cloudflare and saves credentials locally. The tunnel can expose local services to the internet without opening any firewall ports.

commands.cmd: cloudflared tunnel route dns mytunnel app.example.com
commands.desc: Create a CNAME DNS record pointing app.example.com to your Cloudflare Tunnel
commands.tooltip: Creates a CNAME DNS record in Cloudflare pointing your subdomain to the tunnel. Traffic for app.example.com will be routed through the tunnel to your server.

commands.desc: Start the Cloudflare Tunnel daemon — establishes the outbound connection to Cloudflare's edge
commands.tooltip: Starts the tunnel daemon — it establishes an outbound HTTPS connection to Cloudflare's edge servers. No inbound ports need to be open.

commands.desc: List all Cloudflare Tunnels in your account with their status
commands.tooltip: Lists all tunnels in your Cloudflare account with their IDs, creation dates, and active connection status.

commands.desc: Show details about a specific tunnel including active connections
commands.tooltip: Shows the number of active connections for a tunnel and which Cloudflare data center they're connected to.

commands.desc: Delete a Cloudflare Tunnel — removes credentials and DNS routes
commands.tooltip: Permanently deletes a tunnel and its associated DNS routes and credentials.

commands.desc: Install cloudflared as a systemd service so the tunnel starts automatically on boot
commands.tooltip: Installs cloudflared as a systemd service so the tunnel starts automatically on boot without needing to run the command manually.

commands.desc: Generate a 4096-bit RSA private key for self-signed certificates or CSRs
commands.tooltip: Generates a 4096-bit RSA private key. This is the foundation of any TLS certificate — keep this file secret and back it up securely.

commands.desc: Generate a Certificate Signing Request (CSR) from a private key — needed for commercial TLS certificates
commands.tooltip: Creates a Certificate Signing Request containing your domain info and public key. Send this to a commercial CA to get a signed certificate.

commands.desc: Create a self-signed TLS certificate valid for 365 days — useful for internal services and local testing
commands.tooltip: Generates a self-signed certificate that browsers will warn about but which provides encrypted transport. Perfect for internal services.

commands.desc: Verify that a certificate was signed by a specific Certificate Authority
commands.tooltip: Verifies that a certificate's signature chain leads to your CA. Use this to confirm a certificate was signed by the right authority.

commands.desc: Display all details of a certificate — subject, issuer, validity, SANs, and key info
commands.tooltip: Dumps all fields of a certificate in human-readable format — subject, issuer, validity period, Subject Alternative Names, and key algorithm.

commands.desc: Bundle a private key and certificate into a PKCS#12 file — required by some platforms and Java apps
commands.tooltip: Creates a PKCS#12 bundle containing both the certificate and private key in one file. Required by Java keystores and some Windows apps.

commands.desc: Install PostgreSQL, the powerful open-source relational database for self-hosted apps
commands.tooltip: Installs the PostgreSQL database server. PostgreSQL is the default database for many self-hosted apps (Gitea, Nextcloud, Plausible, etc.).

commands.desc: Start the PostgreSQL database server
commands.tooltip: Starts the PostgreSQL service. It runs as the 'postgres' system user and listens on port 5432 by default.

commands.desc: Open the PostgreSQL interactive shell as the postgres superuser
commands.tooltip: Opens the psql interactive shell as the postgres superuser — the admin account that can create databases, users, and grant permissions.

commands.desc: Create a new PostgreSQL database named myapp_db
commands.tooltip: Creates a new PostgreSQL database. Run this before pointing an app at PostgreSQL for the first time.

commands.desc: Create a new PostgreSQL user for an application
commands.tooltip: Creates a new database user. Best practice: give each app its own database user with access only to its own database.

commands.desc: Export a PostgreSQL database to a SQL file — the standard backup method
commands.tooltip: Exports a PostgreSQL database to a plain-text SQL file. Can be restored on any PostgreSQL instance and is human-readable.

commands.desc: Export a PostgreSQL database in custom binary format — faster to restore than plain SQL
commands.tooltip: Exports in PostgreSQL's custom binary format — compressed and faster to restore than plain SQL, but not human-readable.

commands.desc: Restore a PostgreSQL database from a SQL dump file
commands.tooltip: Restores a PostgreSQL database by replaying the SQL commands in the dump file.

commands.desc: Restore a PostgreSQL database from a custom-format dump
commands.tooltip: Restores a database from a custom-format pg_dump file. Supports parallel restore for large databases.

commands.desc: Install MySQL Community Server for self-hosted web applications
commands.tooltip: Installs the MySQL database server. MySQL (or MariaDB) is required by WordPress, Nextcloud (alternatively), and many PHP-based self-hosted apps.

commands.desc: Run the MySQL security hardening wizard — sets root password, removes test databases, and restricts remote root login
commands.tooltip: Interactive post-install wizard that sets the MySQL root password, removes anonymous users, disables remote root login, and drops the test database — run this immediately after install.

commands.desc: Connect to the MySQL server as root with a password prompt
commands.tooltip: Connects to MySQL as root. You'll be prompted for the password set during mysql_secure_installation.

commands.desc: Export a MySQL database to a SQL file for backup
commands.tooltip: Exports a MySQL database to a SQL file. Schedule this in cron for automated backups.

commands.desc: Restore a MySQL database from a SQL dump file
commands.tooltip: Restores a MySQL database from a dump file. Create the database first with 'CREATE DATABASE myapp_db;'.

commands.desc: Export an SQLite database to a SQL text dump — SQLite is used by many lightweight self-hosted apps
commands.tooltip: Exports an SQLite database to SQL text. SQLite is used by many lightweight self-hosted apps including FreshRSS, Gitea (optional), and Home Assistant.

commands.desc: Restore an SQLite database from a SQL dump file
commands.tooltip: Restores an SQLite database from a SQL dump.

commands.desc: Run Prometheus metrics collection server in Docker — the foundation of the self-hosted monitoring stack
commands.tooltip: Runs Prometheus — it scrapes metrics endpoints from your apps and stores them in a time-series database for querying and alerting.

commands.desc: Run Grafana in Docker — connects to Prometheus or other data sources to create dashboards
commands.tooltip: Runs Grafana — connects to Prometheus and lets you build dashboards to visualize CPU, memory, request rates, error counts, and any other metric.

commands.desc: Run Prometheus node_exporter to collect CPU, memory, disk, and network metrics from the host
commands.tooltip: Runs node_exporter which exposes hundreds of host-level metrics (CPU, memory, disk, network) for Prometheus to scrape.

commands.desc: Query the Prometheus API to check which monitored targets are currently up
commands.tooltip: Queries Prometheus to see which scrape targets are reporting as up. The 'up' metric is 1 for healthy targets and 0 for unreachable ones.

commands.desc: Show disk I/O statistics every second for 5 iterations — useful for diagnosing slow database or storage performance
commands.tooltip: Reports disk read/write throughput and utilization for each block device every second. High %util values indicate disk I/O is a bottleneck.

commands.desc: Show how much disk space Docker images, volumes, and containers are consuming
commands.tooltip: Shows Docker's total disk footprint. Docker can accumulate gigabytes of unused images and volumes — run 'docker system prune' if this is large.

commands.desc: Re-run 'df -h' every 5 seconds to monitor disk space in real time
commands.tooltip: Reruns 'df -h' every 5 seconds and refreshes the display — a simple way to watch disk space during a large file operation.

commands.desc: Show the last 20 lines of kernel messages — useful for diagnosing hardware errors, OOM kills, or driver issues
commands.tooltip: Shows the last 20 kernel messages. Look here for hardware errors, Out-Of-Memory (OOM) kill events, or file system errors that cause mysterious crashes.

commands.desc: Incrementally sync a local directory to a remote server — only transfers changed files, with compression
commands.tooltip: Incrementally copies only changed files from /data/ to a remote server. The -a flag preserves permissions, -v is verbose, -z compresses data during transfer.

commands.desc: Sync to remote and delete files on the destination that no longer exist locally — an exact mirror
commands.tooltip: Like rsync -avz but also deletes files at the destination that no longer exist at the source — creates an exact mirror.

commands.desc: Sync a directory to remote while excluding node_modules or other large unneeded folders
commands.tooltip: Excludes specific directories from the sync. Exclude node_modules and .git to keep backups small and fast.

commands.desc: Create a dated gzip-compressed tarball of a directory — a simple scheduled backup pattern
commands.tooltip: Creates a compressed archive with today's date in the filename (e.g. backup-20260414.tar.gz). Schedule this in cron for daily backups.

commands.desc: Extract a compressed tarball to a specific restore directory
commands.tooltip: Extracts a .tar.gz archive to a specific directory. The -C flag specifies the target path.

commands.desc: Cron syntax: run a backup script every day at 2 AM — paste this line inside crontab -e
commands.tooltip: A cron schedule expression meaning 'every day at 2:00 AM'. The five fields are: minute, hour, day-of-month, month, day-of-week.

commands.desc: Install BorgBackup — a deduplicating encrypted backup tool popular for self-hosted server backups
commands.tooltip: Installs BorgBackup — a deduplicating encrypted backup tool. Borg only stores what has changed since the last backup, making large dataset backups very efficient.

commands.desc: Initialize a Borg backup repository on a remote server with encryption
commands.tooltip: Initializes a new encrypted Borg repository on a remote server. The encryption key is stored in the repo (repokey mode) and protected by a passphrase.

commands.desc: Create a new timestamped Borg backup snapshot of the /data directory
commands.tooltip: Creates a new backup snapshot in the Borg repo with an auto-generated name like 'myserver-2026-04-14T02:00'. Only changed blocks are stored.

commands.desc: List all backup archives in a Borg repository
commands.tooltip: Lists every backup snapshot in the Borg repository with its name and timestamp.

commands.desc: Restore a Borg backup archive to the current directory
commands.tooltip: Extracts a backup snapshot to the current directory, recreating the original file structure.

commands.desc: Delete old Borg backups according to a retention policy — keep 7 daily and 4 weekly snapshots
commands.tooltip: Deletes old snapshots according to a retention policy. This example keeps 7 daily and 4 weekly backups and removes everything else.

commands.desc: Install Fail2ban — monitors logs and automatically bans IPs with too many failed login attempts
commands.tooltip: Installs Fail2ban, a daemon that watches log files for repeated failed authentication attempts and bans offending IPs via iptables rules.

commands.desc: Start the Fail2ban service
commands.tooltip: Starts the Fail2ban daemon. Once started it immediately begins monitoring log files defined in its jail configuration.

commands.desc: Show all active Fail2ban jails and the number of banned IPs
commands.tooltip: Shows all active jails and how many IPs are currently banned in each. A quick security audit of what Fail2ban is protecting.

commands.desc: Show detailed status of the SSH jail — how many IPs are currently banned for brute-forcing SSH
commands.tooltip: Detailed status for the SSH jail — total failed attempts, number of current bans, and how long bans last.

commands.desc: Manually unban a specific IP address from the SSH jail
commands.tooltip: Immediately removes a ban. Use this if you accidentally get locked out or if a legitimate user triggers the rate limit.

commands.desc: Configure Fail2ban jails — set ban time, retry limits, and which services to protect
commands.tooltip: The main Fail2ban config file. Set bantime (how long bans last), findtime (window for counting failures), and maxretry (failures before ban). Create this file to override defaults.

commands.desc: Edit the SSH daemon config to harden it — disable root login, change port, require key auth
commands.tooltip: SSH hardening settings live here: set 'PermitRootLogin no', 'PasswordAuthentication no', and optionally change Port from 22 to reduce automated scanning noise.

commands.desc: Test sshd_config for syntax errors before restarting SSH — avoids locking yourself out
commands.tooltip: Tests sshd_config for syntax errors. Run this before restarting sshd — a misconfigured sshd will refuse to start, leaving you unable to SSH in.

commands.desc: Restart the SSH daemon to apply config changes — stay in the current session, it won't disconnect you
commands.tooltip: Applies sshd_config changes. You stay connected in your current session; only new connections use the new config.

commands.desc: Lock the root account's password to prevent direct root login — a basic hardening step
commands.tooltip: Disables password-based root login by locking the root password. Root can still be accessed via 'sudo su' from an admin account.

commands.cmd: sudo apt-get update && sudo apt-get upgrade -y
commands.desc: Update all installed packages to their latest versions — run regularly to get security patches
commands.tooltip: Updates the package list and upgrades all installed software. Run weekly on production servers to stay current with security patches.

commands.desc: Install automatic security update support — keeps the system patched without manual intervention
commands.tooltip: Installs the unattended-upgrades package which automatically applies security updates from Ubuntu's security repository without requiring manual intervention.

commands.desc: Enable or reconfigure automatic security updates interactively
commands.tooltip: Opens a dialog to enable or change the unattended-upgrades configuration — the simplest way to turn on automatic security patching.

commands.desc: Run Lynis — a security auditing tool that scans the system and recommends hardening steps
commands.tooltip: Runs a comprehensive security audit and produces a prioritized list of hardening recommendations specific to your system's current configuration.

commands.desc: Scan for common rootkits and backdoors — a basic intrusion detection check
commands.tooltip: Scans for signs of known rootkits — programs attackers install to maintain persistent hidden access. A quick, basic intrusion detection check.

commands.desc: Run RootKit Hunter to scan for known rootkits, trojans, and suspicious file changes
commands.tooltip: More comprehensive rootkit scan that also checks for suspicious files, wrong permissions, and known malware signatures.

commands.desc: Set correct permissions on the authorized_keys file — SSH will refuse to use it if it's world-readable
commands.tooltip: Sets the authorized_keys file to be readable only by its owner. SSH will silently refuse to use the file if it's writable by other users.

commands.desc: Ensure the SSH daemon config is owned by root and not readable by other users
commands.tooltip: Ensures the SSH config file is owned by root and not readable by other users — prevents local users from reading the SSH configuration.

commands.desc: Create a local .env file from the provided example — then fill in your actual secrets and credentials
commands.tooltip: Most open-source self-hosted apps ship a .env.example file with all required variables listed but values blank. Copy it to .env then fill in your secrets.

commands.desc: Print active .env entries (non-comment, non-blank lines) for a quick review
commands.tooltip: Prints only the active lines from a .env file, filtering out comments and blank lines — a quick way to see what's actually configured.

commands.desc: Edit system-wide environment variables that apply to all users and services
commands.tooltip: System-wide environment variables set here are available to all users and services at login. Use for machine-global settings like JAVA_HOME or LANG.

commands.desc: Create a Docker Swarm secret from a file — secrets are encrypted at rest and only available inside containers that need them
commands.tooltip: Creates a Docker Swarm secret — an encrypted value accessible only inside containers that are explicitly granted permission. More secure than environment variables for passwords.

commands.desc: Pull the latest Immich self-hosted photo management server image from GitHub Container Registry
commands.tooltip: Pulls the latest Immich server image. Immich is a Google Photos alternative with AI-powered search, face recognition, and mobile backup.

commands.desc: Pull the LinuxServer.io Nextcloud image — self-hosted cloud storage, calendar, and contacts
commands.tooltip: Pulls the LinuxServer.io-maintained Nextcloud image. Nextcloud is a self-hosted cloud platform for files, calendars, contacts, and more.

commands.desc: Pull Vaultwarden — a lightweight self-hosted Bitwarden-compatible password manager backend
commands.tooltip: Pulls Vaultwarden — an unofficial Bitwarden-compatible server written in Rust. Lighter and cheaper to run than the official Bitwarden server.

commands.desc: Pull Paperless-ngx — self-hosted document management system with OCR and full-text search
commands.tooltip: Pulls Paperless-ngx — scans, OCRs, tags, and full-text indexes all your documents so you can find any paper you've ever received.

commands.desc: Pull Gitea — a lightweight self-hosted Git service, GitHub alternative
commands.tooltip: Pulls Gitea — a self-hosted Git service with web UI, issue tracker, CI/CD (Gitea Actions), and package registry. Runs on a Raspberry Pi.

commands.desc: Pull Jellyfin — a self-hosted media server for streaming movies, TV, and music
commands.tooltip: Pulls Jellyfin — streams your personal media library to any device, with hardware transcoding support and no subscription or tracking.

commands.desc: Pull Plausible — a lightweight, privacy-friendly self-hosted website analytics alternative to Google Analytics
commands.tooltip: Pulls Plausible Analytics — a GDPR-compliant website analytics tool that doesn't use cookies. A simple, ethical alternative to Google Analytics.

commands.desc: Pull n8n — a self-hosted workflow automation tool, open-source alternative to Zapier
commands.tooltip: Pulls n8n — a visual workflow automation tool with 400+ integrations. Self-host it to automate repetitive tasks without paying per-workflow fees.

commands.desc: Pull Home Assistant — the leading self-hosted smart home automation platform
commands.tooltip: Pulls Home Assistant — connects to 3000+ smart home devices and lets you build complex automations that run locally without cloud dependency.

commands.desc: Pull FreshRSS — a self-hosted RSS feed aggregator
commands.tooltip: Pulls FreshRSS — a self-hosted RSS/Atom aggregator. Keep up with hundreds of websites without algorithmic feeds or ads.

commands.desc: Create a named Docker bridge network so multiple Compose stacks can communicate with each other
commands.tooltip: Creates a named Docker bridge network. Containers on the same named network can reach each other by service name — the basis for multi-stack communication.

commands.desc: Show detailed info about a Docker network including which containers are connected
commands.tooltip: Shows which containers are connected to a network and their assigned IP addresses. Use when a container can't reach another by name.

commands.desc: Show the mount path and details of a specific Docker volume
commands.tooltip: Shows where the volume data is stored on the host filesystem (usually under /var/lib/docker/volumes/) and which containers are using it.

commands.desc: Delete a Docker volume — only run this when you're sure the data inside is no longer needed
commands.tooltip: Deletes a Docker volume permanently. All data inside is gone. Only run this when you've confirmed the data is backed up or no longer needed.

commands.desc: Generate a modern Ed25519 SSH key pair — faster and more secure than RSA for server authentication
commands.tooltip: Generates an Ed25519 SSH key pair — a modern elliptic-curve algorithm that's faster and more secure than RSA. The -C flag adds a comment for identification.

commands.desc: Copy your local public key to a remote server's authorized_keys — enables passwordless SSH login
commands.tooltip: Copies your local public key to the remote server's ~/.ssh/authorized_keys file. After this, you can SSH in without a password.

commands.desc: Connect to a server using a specific private key file
commands.tooltip: Connects to a server using a specific private key file. Use this when you have multiple SSH keys for different servers.

commands.desc: Copy a local directory to a remote server over SSH — simple one-time deployment method
commands.tooltip: Securely copies a local directory to a remote server over SSH. Simple and built-in — useful for initial deployments or small file transfers.

commands.desc: Set the server's hostname — used in logs, prompts, and service discovery
commands.tooltip: Changes the server's hostname — what appears in the terminal prompt, log files, and when other services identify this machine.

commands.desc: Set the server's timezone — critical for log timestamps, cron jobs, and scheduled tasks
commands.tooltip: Sets the system timezone. Critical for log timestamps, cron job timing, and any app that displays local times to users.

commands.desc: Show the current date, time, timezone, and NTP sync status
commands.tooltip: Shows the current date/time, timezone, whether NTP is active, and whether the hardware clock is synced — a complete time configuration summary.

commands.desc: Create a dedicated non-root deployment user — a security best practice for running apps
commands.tooltip: Creates a new standard user account. Best practice: run apps as a non-root user with only the permissions it needs.

commands.desc: Add a user to the docker group so they can run Docker commands without sudo
commands.tooltip: Adds a user to the 'docker' group, granting them permission to run Docker commands without sudo. The -a flag means 'add' (don't replace existing groups).

commands.desc: Grant sudo privileges to a user — needed for system-level operations
commands.tooltip: Grants a user sudo access. They can now run 'sudo command' to execute commands as root after entering their password.

commands.desc: Safely edit the sudoers file which controls who can run which commands with sudo
commands.tooltip: Opens the sudoers file in a protected editor that validates syntax before saving. Never edit /etc/sudoers directly with nano — a syntax error can lock out all sudo access.

commands.cmd: sudo swapoff -a && sudo fallocate -l 2G /swapfile && sudo chmod 600 /swapfile && sudo mkswap /swapfile && sudo swapon /swapfile
commands.desc: Create and enable a 2 GB swap file — helps low-RAM servers avoid OOM kills
commands.tooltip: Creates a 2 GB swap file on servers without a dedicated swap partition. Swap acts as overflow RAM — prevents OOM kills on memory-constrained VPS instances.

commands.desc: Check total RAM, available RAM, and swap size from the kernel's memory stats
commands.tooltip: Reads memory stats directly from the kernel. MemAvailable is more useful than MemFree — it includes memory that can be freed from caches on demand.

commands.desc: Check an app's HTTP health endpoint and print only the status code — useful in scripts and monitoring
commands.tooltip: Returns only the HTTP status code of a health endpoint (e.g., 200 or 503). The -o /dev/null discards the body and -s suppresses progress. Great for monitoring scripts.

commands.desc: Health check that prints UNHEALTHY if the endpoint returns an error — use in Docker health check scripts
commands.tooltip: Health check that prints 'UNHEALTHY' if the endpoint returns a non-200 status or isn't reachable. Use this in Docker health check scripts or CI.

commands.desc: Show the last journal entries with explanations — the best starting point when a service fails to start
commands.tooltip: Shows the end of the system journal with extra explanatory messages. The -x flag adds contextual help text for error messages — start here when a service fails.

commands.desc: Check kernel logs for Out-Of-Memory kills — if your app keeps crashing, this reveals if the kernel is terminating it
commands.tooltip: Searches kernel ring buffer messages for Out-Of-Memory events. If your app keeps crashing without obvious errors, the kernel may be killing it to reclaim memory.

commands.desc: List all processes with open listening network ports — map services to ports
commands.tooltip: Lists all network-listening sockets with their process names and ports in numeric format. A complete picture of what's exposed on the network.

commands.desc: Trace the network path from the server to a destination — diagnose routing issues or high latency hops
commands.tooltip: Shows each hop a network packet takes to reach a destination with its latency. High latency at a specific hop identifies where slowness is introduced.

commands.desc: Run a continuous traceroute that shows packet loss and latency at each hop — better than traceroute for diagnosing network issues
commands.tooltip: Combines ping and traceroute into a live display showing packet loss and round-trip time at every hop. The best tool for diagnosing intermittent network problems.

commands.desc: Ping a known external IP to verify the server has internet connectivity
commands.tooltip: Sends 4 ICMP packets to a known good IP to verify basic internet connectivity. If this fails, the server has no outbound internet access.

commands.desc: Print the server's public IP address — useful when provisioning behind NAT or a load balancer
commands.tooltip: Queries a web service that echoes back the requesting IP. Tells you your server's public IP address as seen from the internet.

commands.desc: Show all network interfaces and their IP addresses on the server
commands.tooltip: Lists all network interfaces (eth0, lo, docker0, wg0, etc.) with their assigned IP addresses, netmasks, and MAC addresses.

commands.desc: Show the server's routing table — check the default gateway and interface routes
commands.tooltip: Displays the kernel routing table — shows which network interface and gateway are used for each destination range. Check here when packets aren't routing correctly.

commands.desc: One-command Dokploy installer — sets up the full stack (Traefik, Docker, dashboard) on a fresh VPS
commands.tooltip: Dokploy's official install script. It installs Docker if not present, pulls the Dokploy server image, configures Traefik as the reverse proxy, and launches the web dashboard on port 3000. Run this on a fresh Ubuntu/Debian VPS as root.

commands.desc: Start the Dokploy server stack manually from its installed Compose file
commands.tooltip: Dokploy runs as a Docker Compose stack. This command brings it up in detached mode. Use this to restart Dokploy after a server reboot or manual stop.

commands.desc: Stop the entire Dokploy server stack
commands.tooltip: Stops all Dokploy containers (dashboard, Traefik, internal services) without deleting volumes. Your deployed apps continue running independently — only the Dokploy management layer stops.

commands.cmd: docker compose -f /etc/dokploy/docker-compose.yml pull && docker compose -f /etc/dokploy/docker-compose.yml up -d
commands.desc: Update Dokploy to the latest version by pulling new images and restarting
commands.tooltip: The standard Dokploy update procedure. Pull latest images first, then recreate containers. Your deployed apps are not affected — only the Dokploy control plane updates.

commands.desc: Stream live logs from the main Dokploy server container
commands.tooltip: View real-time output from the Dokploy backend process. Check here when deployments fail or the dashboard is unresponsive. The container name may vary — use 'docker ps' to confirm.

commands.desc: List all running Dokploy-related containers and their status
commands.tooltip: Filters 'docker ps' to show only containers whose names contain 'dokploy'. Useful for quickly checking whether Dokploy and Traefik are running after a reboot.

commands.desc: View Dokploy's installed Compose configuration — shows all services, volumes, and network settings
commands.tooltip: Dokploy stores its own Compose file at /etc/dokploy/. Reviewing this helps you understand what's running and find volume mount paths for backups or customization.

commands.desc: Edit Dokploy's server environment variables — database URL, secret keys, port settings
commands.tooltip: Dokploy's own runtime configuration lives in this .env file. Modify it to change the dashboard port, enable/disable features, or update the database connection string. Restart Dokploy after changes.

commands.desc: Install the Dokploy CLI tool globally via npm
commands.tooltip: The Dokploy CLI lets you manage your Dokploy server from the command line. After installing, authenticate with 'dokploy auth login' using your server URL and API token.

commands.desc: Authenticate the CLI with your Dokploy server — prompts for server URL and API token
commands.tooltip: Run this once to connect the CLI to your Dokploy instance. You'll need your server's URL (e.g. https://dokploy.example.com) and an API token generated in the Dokploy dashboard under Settings → API.

commands.desc: Remove stored CLI credentials for the current Dokploy server
commands.tooltip: Clears the saved authentication token from your local machine. Run this before switching to a different Dokploy server or when rotating API keys.

commands.desc: Trigger a deployment of the current project from the CLI — reads dokploy.json in the project root
commands.tooltip: Pushes a deployment from your local machine to Dokploy. Requires a dokploy.json config file in the project root specifying the app name and server. Alternatively, deployments are triggered automatically via GitHub webhooks.

commands.desc: List all applications deployed on the Dokploy server
commands.tooltip: Returns a table of all apps with their names, status (running/stopped/error), and URLs. Equivalent to the Apps view in the web dashboard.

commands.desc: Trigger a new deployment for a specific application by name
commands.tooltip: Forces a redeploy of a named app — equivalent to clicking 'Deploy' in the dashboard. Useful for scripting automated deployments from CI/CD pipelines outside of Git webhooks.

commands.desc: Start a stopped application
commands.tooltip: Starts containers for an app that was previously stopped. The app's last deployed image is used — no new build or pull happens.

commands.desc: Stop a running application without deleting it
commands.tooltip: Stops the app's containers. The deployment configuration and volumes are preserved. Useful for temporarily taking an app offline for maintenance.

commands.desc: Restart a running application
commands.tooltip: Sends a stop then start to the app's containers. Useful when an app has become unresponsive or you need to clear its in-memory state.

commands.desc: Stream live logs from a deployed application
commands.tooltip: Tails the stdout/stderr of the app's containers. Equivalent to 'docker logs -f' but accessed through the Dokploy API by app name rather than container ID.

commands.desc: List all environment variables set for a specific application
commands.tooltip: Shows every env var configured for the app in Dokploy — both those set in the dashboard and those inherited from the project's env groups.

commands.desc: Set or update an environment variable for an application
commands.tooltip: Adds or overwrites an environment variable for the named app. The change takes effect on the next deployment — running containers are not automatically updated.

commands.desc: Remove an environment variable from an application
commands.tooltip: Deletes a specific env var from the app's configuration. The deletion takes effect on the next deployment.

commands.desc: List all projects on the Dokploy server
commands.tooltip: Projects in Dokploy are organizational containers that group related apps, databases, and compose stacks together. This lists their names and IDs.

commands.desc: Create a new project on the Dokploy server
commands.tooltip: Projects organize your services. Create one per client, application domain, or environment (staging vs production). Services within the same project share a Docker network by default.

commands.desc: List all services across all projects
commands.tooltip: Shows every service (apps, databases, compose stacks) across all projects with their type and status. More granular than 'dokploy app list' which shows only application-type services.

commands.desc: Deploy or redeploy a Docker Compose stack managed by Dokploy
commands.tooltip: Triggers a fresh deployment of a Compose stack defined in Dokploy. Pulls images and recreates containers according to the latest docker-compose.yml stored in the project.

commands.desc: Start a stopped Compose stack
commands.tooltip: Starts all services in a Dokploy-managed Compose stack. The last deployed configuration is used.

commands.desc: Stop all services in a Compose stack without removing them
commands.tooltip: Stops the stack's containers. Volumes and network configuration are preserved.

commands.desc: List all databases managed by Dokploy across all projects
commands.tooltip: Shows every database service (PostgreSQL, MySQL, MongoDB, Redis, MariaDB) that Dokploy is managing, with their project, type, and status.

commands.desc: Start a stopped database service managed by Dokploy
commands.tooltip: Starts the container for a Dokploy-managed database. The data volume is preserved across stops and starts — no data is lost.

commands.desc: Stop a running database service
commands.tooltip: Gracefully stops the database container. Applications connected to this database will lose their connection until it's restarted.

commands.desc: Trigger a manual backup of a Dokploy-managed database
commands.tooltip: Creates a dump of the database and stores it in the configured backup location. Dokploy supports scheduled automatic backups, but this command creates one on-demand.

commands.desc: Restore a database from a specific backup
commands.tooltip: Restores the database to the state captured in a specific backup. The database is stopped, overwritten with the backup data, then restarted. Destructive — confirm the backup ID with 'dokploy database backup list' first.

commands.desc: Add a custom domain to an application in Dokploy
commands.tooltip: Registers a domain with Dokploy's Traefik router so traffic for app.example.com is routed to the named app. You must also point your DNS A record to the server's IP before requests will work.

commands.desc: List all domains assigned to an application
commands.tooltip: Shows every hostname that routes to this app, along with their HTTPS/certificate status.

commands.desc: Remove a domain from an application
commands.tooltip: Removes the Traefik routing rule for this hostname. Requests to app.example.com will no longer be routed to the app.

commands.desc: Enable automatic Let's Encrypt SSL certificate for an application
commands.tooltip: Instructs Traefik (Dokploy's built-in reverse proxy) to request and auto-renew a free Let's Encrypt certificate for the app's domain. The domain must be publicly reachable on port 80 for the ACME challenge to succeed.

commands.desc: Check the SSL certificate status for an application's domain
commands.tooltip: Shows whether a Let's Encrypt certificate has been issued, its expiry date, and whether Traefik is serving HTTPS traffic for this app.

commands.desc: List all environment variable groups (shared env sets) on the server
commands.tooltip: Env groups in Dokploy let you define a set of variables once and attach them to multiple apps — like a shared .env file for a project. This lists all groups.

commands.desc: Create a new environment variable group
commands.tooltip: Creates a named group for storing shared environment variables. Attach this group to multiple apps to inject the same database URL, API keys, or other shared config values.

commands.desc: Attach an env group to an application so it inherits those variables
commands.tooltip: Merges the group's variables into the app's environment on the next deployment. App-level variables take precedence over group variables if the same key exists in both.

commands.desc: Link an application to a GitHub/GitLab repository and branch for auto-deploy
commands.tooltip: Connects the app to a Git repository. Every push to the specified branch will trigger an automatic build and deployment via Dokploy's webhook. Set up the webhook in your GitHub repo settings using the URL shown in the Dokploy dashboard.

commands.desc: Set the build system Dokploy uses to compile and containerize the application
commands.tooltip: Dokploy supports multiple builders: nixpacks (auto-detects language, no Dockerfile needed), dockerfile (builds from a Dockerfile in the repo), and heroku buildpacks. Nixpacks works out-of-the-box for Node, Python, Ruby, Go, and more.

commands.desc: Configure Dokploy to build the app using a Dockerfile in the repository root
commands.tooltip: Switches the builder from nixpacks to Dockerfile mode. Dokploy will look for a Dockerfile (or Dockerfile.dokploy) in the repository root and build the image from it.

commands.desc: Stream live Traefik logs to debug routing, SSL, and certificate issues
commands.tooltip: Traefik is Dokploy's built-in reverse proxy. Its logs show certificate requests, routing decisions, and errors. Check here when an app's domain isn't resolving or HTTPS isn't working.

commands.desc: Dump Traefik's active routing table to see all Dokploy-managed routes
commands.tooltip: Queries Traefik's dashboard API and pretty-prints the result. Shows every router, service, and middleware currently active — useful for diagnosing misrouted domains.

commands.desc: View Dokploy's Traefik static configuration — entrypoints, cert resolvers, dashboard settings
commands.tooltip: Traefik's static config defines how it listens (ports 80/443), which ACME CA to use for SSL, and whether the dashboard is enabled. Dokploy generates this file — editing it directly will be overwritten on updates.

commands.desc: List Traefik's dynamic configuration files generated by Dokploy for each service
commands.tooltip: Dokploy writes a YAML file here for each service it manages, defining that service's router rules, middleware, and TLS settings. Inspect these to understand exactly how a service is being routed.

commands.desc: Check the Dokploy server health endpoint — returns OK when the API is responsive
commands.tooltip: A quick health check for the Dokploy API server. Returns a 200 OK when the backend is running correctly. Use in uptime monitoring to alert if Dokploy itself goes down.

commands.desc: Show real-time CPU and memory stats for all Dokploy-managed containers
commands.tooltip: Streams live resource metrics for every container in the Dokploy Compose project. Monitor this when investigating high CPU or memory usage across your deployed services.

commands.desc: Show the name and current state of every Dokploy container
commands.tooltip: A quick status check across all Dokploy containers without the full 'docker ps' output. Useful in health check scripts that need a simple running/not-running answer.

commands.desc: List all Docker volumes created and managed by Dokploy
commands.tooltip: Shows volumes that store Dokploy's own data — the database, configuration, certificate store, and Traefik state. Back these up before upgrading Dokploy.

commands.cmd: docker run --rm -v dokploy_dokploy-data:/data -v $(pwd):/backup alpine tar czf /backup/dokploy-backup.tar.gz /data
commands.desc: Create a compressed backup of Dokploy's data volume
commands.tooltip: Mounts Dokploy's data volume into a temporary Alpine container and tars its contents into the current directory. Run this before upgrading Dokploy or migrating to a new server.

commands.desc: Back up Dokploy's entire configuration directory
commands.tooltip: Copies all of Dokploy's config files (Traefik YAML, .env, Compose file) to your home directory. Restore by copying back to /etc/dokploy/ on a new server.

commands.desc: Trigger a deployment via Dokploy's REST API — useful for CI/CD integration
commands.tooltip: Programmatically triggers a deployment without the CLI. Replace <token> with your API token from Dokploy Settings. Integrate this into GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, or any script that needs to deploy after a build.

commands.desc: Fetch all projects and their services via the Dokploy REST API
commands.tooltip: Returns a JSON list of all projects. Use the project IDs returned here for other API calls. The Authorization header must contain your API token from Dokploy Settings → API Tokens.

commands.desc: Install Nixpacks CLI locally to test auto-detection before pushing to Dokploy
commands.tooltip: Nixpacks is the build system Dokploy uses by default. Install it locally to run the same build that Dokploy would run, helping you debug build failures before they happen in production.

commands.desc: Run a local Nixpacks build of the current directory — same process Dokploy uses
commands.tooltip: Builds a Docker image from your project directory using Nixpacks. If this succeeds locally, Dokploy's build should also succeed. If it fails, the error output tells you exactly what went wrong.

commands.desc: Let Nixpacks detect the programming language and build plan for the current directory
commands.tooltip: Prints what language Nixpacks detected (Node, Python, Go, etc.) and which build commands it would run. Useful for verifying auto-detection is correct before deploying.

commands.desc: View the Nixpacks config file that overrides auto-detected build settings for a project
commands.tooltip: nixpacks.toml lets you customize the build: specify the install command, build command, start command, or system packages to add. Create this file in your repo root to override Nixpacks defaults.

commands.desc: List Docker networks created by Dokploy
commands.tooltip: Dokploy creates isolated Docker networks for each project so services can communicate with each other by name. Services in different projects cannot reach each other unless you explicitly connect them.

commands.desc: Show all containers connected to Dokploy's main network and their IPs
commands.tooltip: Displays the full list of containers on Dokploy's shared network. Use this to find IP addresses when debugging inter-service connectivity, or to confirm a service is on the right network.

commands.desc: Verify the Dokploy dashboard is listening on port 3000
commands.tooltip: Confirms the Dokploy web server is bound to port 3000. If this returns no output, the Dokploy container isn't running or failed to start — check 'docker ps' and 'docker logs'.

commands.desc: Open port 3000 in UFW to allow access to the Dokploy dashboard from the internet
commands.tooltip: Required to access the Dokploy web UI remotely. For security, consider restricting this to your IP only: 'sudo ufw allow from YOUR_IP to any port 3000' instead of opening it to everyone.

commands.desc: Close the Dokploy dashboard port after setting up a domain — use the domain instead
commands.tooltip: Once you've configured a custom domain for the Dokploy dashboard (via Traefik), you can close direct port 3000 access. Traffic goes through port 443 on your domain instead, which is more secure.

commands.desc: Migrate Dokploy's configuration to a new server
commands.tooltip: Copies the full Dokploy config directory to a new server. After copying, run the Dokploy installer on the new server to initialize Docker and services, then place this config in /etc/dokploy/ before starting Dokploy.

commands.desc: Open a shell inside the running Dokploy container for internal debugging
commands.tooltip: Direct access to the Dokploy server process environment. Useful for inspecting application state, running database migrations manually, or checking which environment variables are actually set inside Dokploy.

commands.desc: Pull the latest Dokploy server image without updating yet
commands.tooltip: Pre-fetches the newest Dokploy image into Docker's local cache. Pulling and updating in separate steps lets you check the image is available before taking the control plane offline.

commands.desc: Connect to a freshly provisioned VPS as root for the first time
commands.tooltip: First login to a new VPS. Most providers give you root access with a password emailed to you. Immediately create a non-root user and disable password auth after this first login.

commands.desc: Connect to a VPS on the default SSH port 22
commands.tooltip: Standard SSH login. Replace user with your username and YOUR_SERVER_IP with the server's public IP address or hostname.

commands.desc: Connect using a specific SSH private key file instead of password
commands.tooltip: Use this when you have multiple SSH keys. The -i flag specifies which private key to use. This is the standard way to authenticate after setting up key-based auth.

commands.desc: Forward the remote server's PostgreSQL port to your local machine
commands.tooltip: SSH local port forwarding — creates a tunnel so you can connect to the VPS's database from your local machine as if it were running locally. Connect your DB client to localhost:5432 while this tunnel is open.

commands.desc: Forward a remote app port to your local machine for browser testing
commands.tooltip: Tunnels the server's port 3000 to your local port 8080. Open http://localhost:8080 in your browser to access the remote app without opening a public firewall port.

commands.desc: Create a persistent SSH tunnel in the background (-f background, -N no command)
commands.tooltip: The -N flag opens the tunnel without running a remote command. The -f flag backgrounds the process. Useful for long-running tunnels to databases or private services.

commands.desc: Create a non-root user named 'deploy' and grant sudo access — run as root on first login
commands.tooltip: Best practice: never run production apps as root. This creates a dedicated user and adds them to the sudo group. Log in as this user for all future operations.

commands.desc: Create the SSH key directory with correct permissions for a new user
commands.tooltip: SSH requires exact permissions on ~/.ssh and authorized_keys. If these permissions are wrong, SSH silently ignores the keys and falls back to password auth.

commands.desc: Add your public SSH key to authorized_keys to enable passwordless login
commands.tooltip: Paste your public key (from ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub on your local machine) here. After this, SSH will accept your private key instead of asking for a password.

commands.desc: Disable root login via SSH — a critical security hardening step
commands.tooltip: Once you have a sudo user configured, disable root SSH access. This prevents attackers from targeting the root account directly. Run 'sudo systemctl restart sshd' after.

commands.desc: Disable password-based SSH authentication — require keys only
commands.tooltip: Password auth is vulnerable to brute-force attacks. After adding your SSH key, disable passwords so only private key holders can log in. Confirm your key works first or you'll lock yourself out.

commands.cmd: sudo apt update && sudo apt upgrade -y && sudo apt autoremove -y
commands.desc: Update all packages and remove unused dependencies in one command
commands.tooltip: Run this immediately after provisioning a new VPS and regularly thereafter. 'update' refreshes the package list, 'upgrade' installs updates, 'autoremove' cleans orphaned packages.

commands.cmd: sudo apt install -y curl wget git unzip htop ufw fail2ban
commands.desc: Install a standard toolkit of utilities needed on most VPS servers
commands.tooltip: These are the essential packages for any VPS: curl/wget for downloading, git for version control, unzip for archives, htop for monitoring, ufw for firewall, and fail2ban for brute-force protection.

commands.desc: Simulate automatic security updates to see what would be upgraded
commands.tooltip: Tests the unattended-upgrades configuration without actually installing anything. Run after setup to confirm security patches will be applied automatically.

commands.desc: Fix interrupted or broken package installations
commands.tooltip: When an apt install is interrupted (power loss, Ctrl+C), packages can be left in a half-installed state. This command completes any pending configurations and usually fixes 'dpkg was interrupted' errors.

commands.desc: Install tools to manage additional APT repositories (PPAs)
commands.tooltip: Required before you can add third-party repositories with 'add-apt-repository'. Many tools (Docker, Node.js, etc.) provide their own repos for up-to-date packages.

commands.desc: Get the server's public IPv4 address from an external service
commands.tooltip: Queries ifconfig.me and returns only the IPv4 address. Useful after provisioning to confirm the public IP or in scripts that need to know the external address.

commands.desc: Get the server's public IPv6 address
commands.tooltip: Returns the server's IPv6 address. Many VPS providers now assign IPv6 by default. Use this to verify IPv6 connectivity and get the address for DNS AAAA records.

commands.desc: Apply network configuration changes on Ubuntu 18.04+ without rebooting
commands.tooltip: Ubuntu uses Netplan to manage network configuration. After editing /etc/netplan/*.yaml, run this to apply changes immediately. Without it, changes only take effect on reboot.

commands.desc: Edit Ubuntu's Netplan network configuration file — IP, gateway, DNS settings
commands.tooltip: This YAML file defines the server's network interfaces, static IPs, routes, and DNS servers. Edit carefully — a mistake here can make the server unreachable. Always test with 'netplan try' first.

commands.desc: Test a Netplan config change — auto-reverts after 2 minutes if not confirmed
commands.tooltip: Safer than 'netplan apply' — applies the config but automatically reverts if you don't confirm within 120 seconds. Protects you from locking yourself out with a bad network config.

commands.desc: Query DNS to resolve a hostname — verify DNS resolver is working
commands.tooltip: Asks the configured DNS server to resolve google.com. If this fails, check /etc/resolv.conf for the nameserver entries.

commands.desc: Compact view of listening ports and their processes — cleaner output
commands.tooltip: Trims 'ss -tlnp' output to just the port and process columns. Easy to scan for unexpected services or confirm a newly started service is bound to the right port.

commands.desc: Show disk usage with clear column headers in human-readable sizes
commands.tooltip: Cleaner version of 'df -h'. Shows device, total size, used, available, percentage used, and mount point. Monitor the root (/) filesystem — when it fills up, services stop working.

commands.desc: Find the 20 largest top-level directories on the server by disk usage
commands.tooltip: Scans the root filesystem and shows the biggest space consumers. Common culprits: /var/log (large logs), /var/lib/docker (images and volumes), /home (user data), /tmp (leftover files).

commands.desc: Show disk I/O utilization for each block device — 3 samples, 1 second apart
commands.tooltip: Reports read/write throughput and %util for each disk. High %util (near 100%) on the main disk indicates I/O saturation — the disk is the bottleneck for application performance.

commands.desc: Show a full memory statistics summary — useful for understanding swap usage
commands.tooltip: One-shot view of virtual memory stats: total/used RAM, swap, page-in/page-out activity. High 'swapped in' or 'paged out' values indicate memory pressure.

commands.desc: Read the kernel's 1/5/15 minute load averages and running process count
commands.tooltip: Load average represents how many processes are waiting for CPU time. Values above the number of CPU cores indicate the system is overloaded. Check 'nproc' for your core count.

commands.desc: Show detailed partition table for all disks including unformatted volumes
commands.tooltip: Low-level view of all storage devices and their partition tables. Shows unformatted disks that 'df' won't show. Use when a new volume isn't appearing after attaching it in your VPS provider's dashboard.

commands.desc: Format a new disk or volume with the ext4 filesystem
commands.tooltip: Run this on a newly attached volume before you can mount it. /dev/vdb is the typical name for the second disk on KVM-based VPS providers. Replace with the correct device from 'lsblk'.

commands.desc: Mount a formatted disk to the /data directory
commands.tooltip: Makes the disk accessible at /data. This mount is temporary — it disappears on reboot unless added to /etc/fstab. Create /data first with 'sudo mkdir -p /data'.

commands.desc: Add a disk to /etc/fstab so it mounts automatically on every reboot
commands.tooltip: fstab entries persist mounts across reboots. The fields are: device, mount point, filesystem, options, dump, fsck priority. Test with 'sudo mount -a' to catch errors before rebooting.

commands.desc: Mount all filesystems listed in /etc/fstab — test for errors after editing
commands.tooltip: Attempts to mount everything in /etc/fstab. Run this after adding a new entry to check for errors without rebooting. A bad fstab entry can prevent the server from booting.

commands.desc: Expand an ext4 filesystem to fill the full partition after resizing the disk at the VPS provider
commands.tooltip: After you increase a disk's size in your VPS provider's dashboard, the filesystem doesn't automatically expand. Run 'sudo growpart /dev/vda 1' first to grow the partition, then this to expand the filesystem.

commands.cmd: sudo fallocate -l 2G /swapfile && sudo chmod 600 /swapfile && sudo mkswap /swapfile && sudo swapon /swapfile
commands.desc: Create and activate a 2 GB swapfile in one command chain
commands.tooltip: Essential on VPS instances with 1-2 GB RAM. Swap prevents the OOM killer from terminating your apps under memory pressure. The 600 permission is required — a world-readable swapfile is a security risk.

commands.cmd: echo '/swapfile none swap sw 0 0' | sudo tee -a /etc/fstab
commands.desc: Make the swapfile persistent across reboots by adding it to /etc/fstab
commands.tooltip: Without this, the swapfile is inactive after each reboot. Add this line to /etc/fstab to automatically activate swap on startup.

commands.desc: Show active swap spaces — file, type, size, and used amount
commands.tooltip: Confirms that swap is active and shows how much is in use. If this returns nothing, swap is not configured. Compare with 'free -h' which shows the same info differently.

commands.desc: Show the kernel's swappiness setting — how aggressively it swaps memory to disk
commands.tooltip: Swappiness ranges from 0 (avoid swapping, prefer RAM) to 100 (swap aggressively). The default is 60. For VPS servers running apps that need fast response, lower values like 10 are recommended.

commands.desc: Reduce swappiness to 10 — tell the kernel to prefer RAM over swap
commands.tooltip: Sets swappiness immediately without a reboot. Add 'vm.swappiness=10' to /etc/sysctl.conf to make it permanent. Lower swappiness improves application response time by avoiding disk-based swap usage.

commands.desc: Show all error-level messages from the current boot
commands.tooltip: Filters systemd's journal to error and above since the last boot. The fastest way to find what went wrong after an unexpected reboot or service failure.

commands.desc: Show how much disk space the systemd journal is consuming
commands.tooltip: Journal logs can grow large on busy servers. If disk space is tight, check this first. Use 'sudo journalctl --vacuum-time=7d' to delete logs older than 7 days.

commands.desc: Delete systemd journal logs older than 7 days to free disk space
commands.tooltip: Prunes old journal entries. Safe to run on any server — the journal automatically reopens new log files. Adjust the time period (7d, 2weeks, 1month) based on your retention needs.

commands.desc: Follow the system-wide syslog in real time for general system events
commands.tooltip: syslog captures messages from the kernel, services, and applications. Not as structured as journalctl but universally available. Useful when journalctl isn't finding what you need.

commands.desc: Monitor SSH login attempts and sudo usage in real time
commands.tooltip: Shows every SSH login attempt (successful and failed) and every sudo command run. Essential for security monitoring — watch for repeated failed logins from unknown IPs.

commands.desc: Show the 20 most recent failed SSH password attempts
commands.tooltip: Reveals brute-force attempts against your server. If you see thousands of failures from external IPs, ensure password auth is disabled and Fail2ban is running.

commands.desc: Show the 20 most recent failed login attempts
commands.tooltip: Complements 'last' by showing unsuccessful logins. Requires root. A large number of entries from external IPs indicates active brute-force scanning.

commands.desc: Increase the maximum socket connection backlog for high-traffic servers
commands.tooltip: The default connection queue (128) can cause connection drops under heavy load. This raises it to 65535. Also set net.ipv4.tcp_max_syn_backlog=65535 and the corresponding app-level setting (e.g., Nginx worker_connections).

commands.desc: Increase the system-wide limit on open file descriptors
commands.tooltip: Each network connection, open file, and socket uses a file descriptor. Docker-heavy servers and high-traffic apps can exhaust the default limit (65536). Set this and also increase per-process limits in /etc/security/limits.conf.

commands.desc: Show the current shell's open file descriptor limit
commands.tooltip: Returns the per-process file descriptor limit for the current shell. If this is below 65536, services that open many connections (Nginx, Node.js, databases) may hit 'too many open files' errors.

commands.desc: Edit persistent kernel parameter settings that survive reboots
commands.tooltip: Add sysctl settings here to persist them. Each line is a key=value pair. Apply immediately with 'sudo sysctl -p'. Common tuning parameters: net.ipv4.tcp_tw_reuse, vm.swappiness, fs.inotify.max_user_watches.

commands.desc: Apply all settings from /etc/sysctl.conf without rebooting
commands.tooltip: Reloads sysctl settings from the config file. Run this after editing /etc/sysctl.conf to apply changes immediately.

commands.desc: Show the 15 processes consuming the most memory
commands.tooltip: Sorts processes by memory usage (RSS) descending. Use this when the server is memory-constrained to find which process to scale down, optimize, or kill.

commands.desc: Show the 15 processes consuming the most CPU
commands.tooltip: Sorts processes by CPU usage descending. Use this during high CPU situations to identify the offending process before taking action.

commands.desc: Forcefully terminate a process by PID when normal kill doesn't work
commands.tooltip: SIGKILL (-9) immediately terminates the process without giving it a chance to clean up. Use as a last resort — the process cannot ignore this signal. Find the PID with 'ps aux' or 'pgrep processname'.

commands.desc: Kill all processes matching a command string pattern
commands.tooltip: More convenient than kill when you don't know the PID. The -f flag matches against the full command line. Add -SIGTERM first for a graceful shutdown before escalating to kill -9.

commands.desc: Run a CPU-intensive script at lower priority so it doesn't impact other services
commands.tooltip: Sets the process scheduling priority. Nice values range from -20 (highest priority) to 19 (lowest). Setting +10 means the process yields CPU to other processes. Useful for backup scripts and batch jobs.

commands.desc: Lower the CPU priority of an already-running process
commands.tooltip: Changes the nice value of a running process without restarting it. Use when you find a process consuming too much CPU but can't restart it right now.

commands.desc: Gracefully reboot the VPS — applies kernel updates and clears memory
commands.tooltip: Reboots cleanly after giving running services time to stop. Run after kernel updates (shown by 'needs-restarting -r' or a 'reboot required' message) to apply them.

commands.desc: Immediately power off the VPS
commands.tooltip: Halts the server immediately after syncing disks. Use before taking a snapshot at your VPS provider for a clean, consistent state.

commands.cmd: sudo shutdown -r +5 'Rebooting for kernel update in 5 minutes'
commands.desc: Schedule a reboot in 5 minutes with a broadcast message to logged-in users
commands.tooltip: The +5 means reboot in 5 minutes. The message is broadcast to all logged-in terminal sessions. Gives connected users time to save work.

commands.desc: Check whether a reboot is required to apply installed updates (RHEL/CentOS)
commands.tooltip: On RHEL-based systems, this checks if the running kernel matches the installed kernel. Not available on Ubuntu — check /var/run/reboot-required instead.

commands.desc: Check if Ubuntu requires a reboot to apply a kernel or library update
commands.tooltip: Ubuntu creates this file when an update requires a reboot (usually a kernel or glibc update). If this file exists, schedule a reboot soon.

commands.desc: Shut down the VPS cleanly — best state for taking a provider snapshot
commands.tooltip: Powers off the server after syncing disks. Take VPS snapshots while the server is off for the most consistent backup state — avoids capturing in-flight writes.

commands.desc: Install cloud disk utilities — includes growpart for resizing partitions after VPS disk expansion
commands.tooltip: This package provides 'growpart' which resizes a partition to fill the available disk space. Run it after expanding your VPS disk in the provider dashboard, then follow with 'resize2fs'.

commands.desc: Expand the first partition on vda to fill the entire resized disk
commands.tooltip: After resizing your disk in the VPS dashboard, the partition still has the old size. 'growpart' expands it. Then run 'sudo resize2fs /dev/vda1' to expand the filesystem into the new partition space.

commands.desc: Add a third-party security hardening repository to APT
commands.tooltip: Example of adding an external APT repository. Many VPS security tools, monitoring agents, and OS add-ons are distributed this way. Always verify the URL and publisher before piping install scripts to bash.

commands.cmd: dd if=/dev/zero of=/tmp/testfile bs=1G count=1 oflag=dsync
commands.desc: Benchmark disk write speed by writing 1 GB of zeros with fsync
commands.tooltip: A quick disk I/O benchmark. The oflag=dsync forces synchronous writes for an accurate measurement. Delete /tmp/testfile afterward. Typical VPS NVMe speeds are 200-2000 MB/s; HDD VPS are 50-150 MB/s.

commands.desc: Run a network bandwidth speed test from the VPS
commands.tooltip: Tests the server's network speed to Speedtest.net servers. Tells you the actual bandwidth your VPS has to the internet — useful for comparing with your provider's advertised specs.

commands.desc: Run 10-cycle network path trace to 8.8.8.8 and print a report
commands.tooltip: Combines ping and traceroute. Shows packet loss and latency at every hop between your VPS and the destination. Use this to diagnose network issues with your VPS provider's infrastructure.

commands.desc: Benchmark CPU performance by computing prime numbers up to 20000
commands.tooltip: Sysbench is a general-purpose benchmarking tool. This CPU test measures single-threaded performance. Compare total time and events/sec across VPS sizes or providers.

commands.desc: Show a short summary of the server's hardware — CPU, RAM, network, disks
commands.tooltip: Lists hardware components detected by the system. On a VPS this shows the virtual hardware the hypervisor presents: virtualized CPU, RAM, NIC model, and storage controllers.
